flashcards.front: What are the disadvantages of Married Filing Separately (MFS)?
flashcards.back:
**Limited/Lost Benefits:**
• Child tax credit reduced
• No EITC
• No education credits
• No student loan interest deduction
• Lower IRA phase-outs
• Higher capital gains rates apply sooner
• Social Security benefits may be taxable

**Both must itemize or both use standard deduction**

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for Qualifying Surviving Spouse status?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Spouse died in prior 2 years
• Did not remarry before year-end
• Maintains home for dependent child
• Child lived in home all year
• Entitled to claim child as dependent

**Benefits:** Uses MFJ tax brackets and standard deduction

**Available:** Year of death (as MFJ) + 2 following years

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for Head of Household (HOH) status?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Unmarried (or considered unmarried) at year-end
• Paid >50% of household costs
• Qualifying person lived with taxpayer >6 months (parent exception)

**Qualifying Person:**
• Qualifying child
• Qualifying relative who is close family member
• Dependent parent (doesn\

flashcards.front: What fringe benefits are excludable from gross income?
flashcards.back:
**Tax-Free Fringe Benefits:**
• Health insurance premiums (employer-paid)
• Group term life insurance (first $50K)
• De minimis benefits (small value)
• Working condition fringe (job-related)
• Qualified transportation ($315/month 2024)
• Qualified moving (military only)
• Dependent care assistance ($5,000)
• Educational assistance ($5,250)
• Employee discounts (up to limits)

flashcards.front: What are the rules for tip income reporting?
flashcards.back:
**Employee Responsibilities:**
• Report all tips to employer
• File Form 4070 if tips ≥ $20/month
• Report on Form 1040 even if not reported to employer

**Allocated Tips:**
• Large food/beverage establishments
• Allocated if reported tips < 8% of sales
• Shown on W-2 Box 8
• Not included in Box 1

flashcards.front: When are scholarships and fellowships taxable?
flashcards.back:
**Tax-Free if used for:**
• Tuition and fees
• Books, supplies, equipment required for courses

**Taxable:**
• Room and board
• Travel
• Optional equipment
• Amounts for teaching, research, or services

**Must be degree candidate at eligible institution**

flashcards.front: How is alimony treated for divorces before vs after 2019?
flashcards.back:
**Pre-2019 Divorces:**
• Deductible by payor (above-the-line)
• Taxable to recipient
• Must meet IRC §71 requirements

**Post-2018 Divorces (TCJA):**
• NOT deductible by payor
• NOT taxable to recipient
• Treated like child support

**Modification:** Pre-2019 agreements can elect new rules

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for the Section 121 home sale exclusion?
flashcards.back:
**Ownership and Use Tests:**
• Owned home 2+ years in last 5 years
• Used as principal residence 2+ years in last 5 years
• Not used exclusion in prior 2 years

**Exclusion Amount:**
• $250,000 single
• $500,000 MFJ (both must meet use test, one meets ownership)

**Reduced exclusion available for job change, health, unforeseen circumstances**

flashcards.front: How do you calculate Adjusted Basis of property?
flashcards.back:
**Adjusted Basis = Original Basis ± Adjustments**

**Increases:**
• Capital improvements
• Assessments (sidewalks, utilities)
• Restoration after casualty

**Decreases:**
• Depreciation allowed or allowable
• Casualty/theft losses claimed
• Insurance reimbursements
• Easement payments received

flashcards.front: What are the key rules for Like-Kind Exchanges (Section 1031)?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Real property for real property (post-TCJA)
• Held for investment or business use
• Same taxpayer on both ends

**Timelines:**
• 45 days to identify replacement property
• 180 days to complete exchange

**Boot:** Cash or unlike property received is taxable

**Basis:** Carryover + boot paid - boot received

flashcards.front: How are installment sales taxed?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:** Report gain as payments received

**Gross Profit Percentage:**
Gross Profit ÷ Contract Price

**Each Payment:**
Payment × GP% = Taxable Gain

**Cannot Use For:**
• Inventory
• Depreciation recapture (recognized immediately)
• Related party sales (certain)

**Interest must be charged or imputed**

flashcards.front: What is the Educator Expense deduction?
flashcards.back:
**Amount:** Up to $300 per educator (2024)

**Requirements:**
• K-12 teacher, instructor, counselor, principal, aide
• At least 900 hours during school year
• Unreimbursed classroom expenses

**Eligible Expenses:**
• Books, supplies
• Computer equipment
• COVID-19 protective items
• Professional development courses

flashcards.front: What are the rules for HSA contributions?
flashcards.back:
**2024 Limits:**
• Self-only: $4,150
• Family: $8,300
• Catch-up (55+): +$1,000

**Requirements:**
• HDHP coverage
• No other health coverage (exceptions)
• Not Medicare eligible
• Not a dependent

**Triple Tax Benefit:**
• Deductible contribution
• Tax-free growth
• Tax-free qualified distribution

flashcards.front: How is the Self-Employment Tax calculated?
flashcards.back:
**SE Tax = Net SE Income × 92.35% × 15.3%**

**Rates (2024):**
• Social Security: 12.4% up to $168,600
• Medicare: 2.9% (no cap)
• Additional Medicare: 0.9% over $200K/$250K

**Deduction:**
50% of SE tax is above-the-line deduction

**Net SE Income = Schedule C profit - above-the-line deductions**

flashcards.front: What are the rules for deducting student loan interest?
flashcards.back:
**Maximum Deduction:** $2,500

**Phase-out (2024 MAGI):**
• Single: $75,000 - $90,000
• MFJ: $155,000 - $185,000

**Requirements:**
• Paid by taxpayer legally obligated on loan
• Qualified education expenses
• Cannot be claimed as dependent
• Cannot file MFS

**Above-the-line deduction**

flashcards.front: What expenses qualify for the medical expense deduction?
flashcards.back:
**Deductible Medical Expenses:**
• Doctor, dentist, hospital
• Prescription drugs
• Health insurance premiums (not pre-tax)
• Long-term care (per age limits)
• Medical equipment
• Transportation for medical care
• Hearing aids, glasses

**NOT Deductible:**
• Cosmetic surgery
• Gym memberships
• Nonprescription drugs (exceptions)

flashcards.front: What are the rules for deducting State and Local Taxes (SALT)?
flashcards.back:
**$10,000 Cap** ($5,000 MFS)

**Deductible Taxes:**
• State/local income taxes OR sales taxes
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes (based on value)

**NOT Deductible:**
• Federal taxes
• Foreign income taxes (credit instead)
• Estate, gift, inheritance taxes
• Special assessments

flashcards.front: What are the rules for deducting home mortgage interest?
flashcards.back:
**Acquisition Debt Limit:**
• $750,000 (post-12/15/2017)
• $1,000,000 (grandfathered pre-12/16/2017)

**Deductible on:**
• Main home + one second home
• Points (amortized for refi)

**Home Equity Debt:**
Only deductible if used to buy, build, or substantially improve home

**MFS: $375,000 limit**

flashcards.front: What substantiation is required for charitable contribution deductions?
flashcards.back:
**Cash Contributions:**
• <$250: Bank record or receipt
• ≥$250: Written acknowledgment from charity

**Noncash Contributions:**
• >$250: Written acknowledgment
• >$500: Form 8283 Section A
• >$5,000: Qualified appraisal + 8283 Section B
• >$500,000: Attach appraisal to return

**Vehicle over $500: Form 1098-C**

flashcards.front: What are the AGI limits for charitable contribution deductions?
flashcards.back:
**Cash to Public Charities:** 60% AGI

**Capital Gain Property to Public:** 30% AGI

**Cash to Private Foundations:** 30% AGI

**Capital Gain Property to Private:** 20% AGI

**Carryover:** 5 years for excess contributions

**Ordering:** Current year contributions first, then carryovers (FIFO)

flashcards.front: What are the rules for the Child Tax Credit (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Credit Amount:** $2,000 per qualifying child

**Qualifying Child:**
• Under age 17 at year-end
• SSN required
• Must be dependent

**Phase-out:**
• Begins: $200K ($400K MFJ)
• Reduction: $50 per $1,000 over threshold

**Refundable portion:** Up to $1,700 (Additional CTC)

flashcards.front: What is the Credit for Other Dependents (ODC)?
flashcards.back:
**Credit Amount:** $500 per dependent

**Applies to:**
• Qualifying children 17+ (in college)
• Qualifying relatives
• Dependents with ITIN

**Phase-out:**
Same as CTC ($200K/$400K)

**Nonrefundable credit**

flashcards.front: What are the key EITC requirements and amounts (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Earned income
• Investment income < $11,600
• US citizen/resident alien
• Valid SSN
• Cannot file MFS

**Maximum Credit 2024:**
• 0 children: ~$632
• 1 child: ~$4,213
• 2 children: ~$6,960
• 3+ children: ~$7,830

**Fully refundable**

flashcards.front: What are the differences between AOTC and Lifetime Learning Credit?
flashcards.back:
**AOTC:**
• $2,500 max (100% of $2K + 25% of next $2K)
• First 4 years undergraduate
• Half-time enrollment required
• 40% refundable ($1,000)
• No felony drug conviction

**LLC:**
• $2,000 max (20% of $10,000)
• Any course (undergrad, grad, job skills)
• No enrollment requirement
• Nonrefundable
• Drug conviction OK

flashcards.front: How does the Child and Dependent Care Credit work?
flashcards.back:
**Expense Limit:**
• $3,000 for one qualifying person
• $6,000 for two+ qualifying persons

**Credit Rate:** 20-35% based on AGI
• 35% for AGI ≤ $15,000
• Decreases 1% per $2,000 AGI
• Floors at 20% (AGI > $43,000)

**Purpose:** Work-related child/dependent care

**Nonrefundable**

flashcards.front: What is the Retirement Savings Contributions Credit?
flashcards.back:
**Credit Rate:** 10%, 20%, or 50% of contributions

**Max Contribution Considered:** $2,000
Max Credit: $1,000 (50% × $2,000)

**AGI Limits 2024:**
• 50%: ≤$23K (S), ≤$46K (MFJ)
• 20%: ≤$25K (S), ≤$50K (MFJ)
• 10%: ≤$38K (S), ≤$76K (MFJ)

**Must be 18+, not student or dependent**

flashcards.front: When is a Traditional IRA contribution deductible?
flashcards.back:
**Fully deductible if:**
• No employer plan coverage, OR
• Covered + income below phase-out:
  - Single: $77,000-$87,000 MAGI
  - MFJ (contributor covered): $123K-$143K
  - MFJ (spouse covered): $230K-$240K

**2024 Limit:** $7,000 (+$1,000 catch-up 50+)

**Can always contribute; deduction may be limited**

flashcards.front: What are the Roth IRA contribution limits and phase-outs?
flashcards.back:
**2024 Contribution Limit:** $7,000 (+$1,000 catch-up 50+)

**Phase-out (MAGI):**
• Single: $146,000-$161,000
• MFJ: $230,000-$240,000

**Key Benefits:**
• Tax-free growth
• Tax-free qualified distributions
• No RMDs during owner\

flashcards.front: What are exceptions to the 10% early distribution penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Exceptions (from any plan):**
• Death
• Disability
• Substantially Equal Periodic Payments (SEPP)
• Medical expenses > 7.5% AGI
• IRS levy

**IRA-Only Exceptions:**
• First-time home ($10K lifetime)
• Qualified higher education
• Health insurance (if unemployed)
• Birth/adoption ($5K)

**401(k) additional:** Age 55 separation from service

flashcards.front: What are the RMD rules after SECURE 2.0?
flashcards.back:
**RMD Starting Age:**
• Born before 1951: 72
• Born 1951-1959: 73
• Born 1960+: 75

**Calculation:**
RMD = Account Balance (Dec 31 prior year) ÷ Life Expectancy Factor

**Penalty:** 25% of missed amount (10% if corrected timely)

**Roth IRA:** No RMDs during owner\

flashcards.front: What are the AMT exemption amounts and rates (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Exemption Amounts:**
• Single: $85,700
• MFJ: $133,300
• MFS: $66,650

**Phase-out Starts:**
• Single: $609,350
• MFJ: $1,218,700

**Tax Rates:**
• 26% on first $232,600 ($116,300 MFS)
• 28% on amounts above

flashcards.front: What are common AMT adjustments and preferences?
flashcards.back:
**Common Adjustments (Add Back):**
• State/local taxes (SALT)
• Misc. itemized deductions
• Personal exemptions (if applicable)

**Preference Items:**
• Private activity bond interest
• ISO exercise spread

**NOT Adjusted:**
• Charitable contributions
• Medical expenses (10% threshold instead of 7.5%)
• Mortgage interest (acquisition debt)

flashcards.front: What are the safe harbors to avoid the underpayment penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Safe Harbors (owe <$1,000 OR):**

**Pay at least:**
• 90% of current year tax liability, OR
• 100% of prior year tax (110% if AGI >$150K/$75K MFS)

**Estimated Payments Due:**
• Q1: April 15
• Q2: June 15
• Q3: September 15
• Q4: January 15 (following year)

**Calendar year basis; varies for fiscal year**

flashcards.front: What is the Annualized Income Installment Method?
flashcards.back:
**Purpose:** Reduces penalty when income varies throughout year

**Method:**
• Annualize income through each period
• Calculate tax on annualized amount
• Required installment = Annualized tax ÷ 4

**Periods:**
• Q1: Jan-Mar (annualize × 4)
• Q2: Jan-May (annualize × 12/5)
• Q3: Jan-Aug (annualize × 12/8)
• Q4: Full year

flashcards.front: What is the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion?
flashcards.back:
**2024 Exclusion:** $126,500

**Requirements:**
• Foreign earned income
• Tax home in foreign country
• Meet Physical Presence (330 days) OR Bona Fide Residence test

**Housing Exclusion:**
• Additional exclusion/deduction
• For housing costs over base amount

**Form 2555 required**

flashcards.front: How does the Foreign Tax Credit work?
flashcards.back:
**Credit for:**
Foreign income taxes paid or accrued

**Limitation:**
FTC Limit = US Tax × (Foreign Source Income / Worldwide Income)

**Carry Rules:**
• Back 1 year
• Forward 10 years

**Form 1116 required** (generally)

**Alternative:** Deduct as itemized deduction (usually less beneficial)
flashcards.formula: FTC Limit = US Tax × (Foreign Income / Total Income)

flashcards.front: How are Social Security benefits taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Provisional Income = AGI + ½ SS + Tax-exempt interest**

**Single:**
• <$25K: 0% taxable
• $25K-$34K: Up to 50% taxable
• >$34K: Up to 85% taxable

**MFJ:**
• <$32K: 0% taxable
• $32K-$44K: Up to 50% taxable
• >$44K: Up to 85% taxable

flashcards.front: What are the Kiddie Tax rules?
flashcards.back:
**Applies to:**
• Children under 19
• Students under 24
• Who have unearned income > threshold

**2024 Threshold:** $2,600

**Tax Rate:** Parent\

flashcards.front: What are the tax rules for 529 Plans?
flashcards.back:
**Contributions:**
• Not federally deductible
• Gift tax annual exclusion applies
• 5-year superfunding allowed

**Distributions:**
• Tax-free for qualified education expenses
• Tuition, fees, books, room & board, computers
• K-12 tuition: $10,000/year limit

**Excess/Non-qualified:**
• Earnings taxable + 10% penalty

**SECURE 2.0:** Roth rollover option (up to $35K lifetime)

flashcards.front: What are the rules for Coverdell Education Savings Accounts?
flashcards.back:
**Contribution Limit:** $2,000/year per beneficiary

**Phase-out (MAGI):**
• Single: $95K-$110K
• MFJ: $190K-$220K

**Qualified Expenses:**
• K-12 and higher education
• Broader than 529

**Must use by age 30** (or roll to family member)

**Tax-free if qualified**

flashcards.front: What is the failure to file penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 5% of unpaid tax per month (or partial month)

**Maximum:** 25% of unpaid tax

**Minimum:** If >60 days late: Lesser of $510 or 100% of tax due

**Fraudulent Failure:** 15% per month, up to 75%

**Combined with late payment:** Reduced to 4.5% (0.5% for payment applies)

flashcards.front: What is the failure to pay penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 0.5% of unpaid tax per month

**Maximum:** 25% of unpaid tax

**Rate Increases:** To 1% per month if not paid within 10 days of IRS notice of levy

**Rate Decreases:** To 0.25% if installment agreement in place

**Reasonable cause can abate penalty**

flashcards.front: What are the accuracy-related penalties?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 20% of underpayment

**Applies to:**
• Negligence
• Disregard of rules
• Substantial understatement (>10% and >$5K, or >$10K for corps)
• Substantial valuation misstatement

**Increased to 40%:**
• Gross valuation misstatement

**Defense:** Reasonable cause and good faith

flashcards.front: What is the civil fraud penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 75% of underpayment due to fraud

**Burden of Proof:** IRS must prove fraud by clear and convincing evidence

**Badges of Fraud:**
• False documents
• Concealment of income
• Multiple returns
• Fictitious deductions

**Cannot stack with accuracy penalties on same underpayment**

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 filing thresholds for individuals?
flashcards.back:
**Under 65 (Generally):**
• Single: $14,600
• MFJ: $29,200
• HOH: $21,900
• MFS: $5 (always file)

**65 or Older:**
• Single: $16,550
• MFJ (both): $32,300

**Must File if:**
• SE income ≥ $400
• Owe special taxes (AMT, household employment)
• Received ACA advance payments

flashcards.front: How do individual tax return extensions work?
flashcards.back:
**Form 4868:** Automatic 6-month extension to file

**Extended Deadline:** October 15

**Important:**
• Extension to FILE, not to PAY
• Must pay estimated tax by April 15
• Penalty and interest on late payments

**Combat Zone:** Additional time beyond 6 months

**Cannot extend estimated tax deadlines**

flashcards.front: How is gambling income and losses reported?
flashcards.back:
**Winnings:**
• Fully taxable as ordinary income
• Reported on Form W-2G if over thresholds
• Withholding may apply

**Losses:**
• Deductible only as itemized deduction
• Limited to winnings
• Must maintain documentation

**Professional Gamblers:**
Schedule C income; losses allowed against winnings

flashcards.front: How are virtual currency transactions taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Property Treatment:**
• Sale = Capital gain/loss
• Mining = Ordinary income (FMV when received)
• Payment for services = Ordinary income

**Basis:** Amount paid (including fees)

**Holding Period:**
• >1 year: Long-term capital gain
• ≤1 year: Short-term (ordinary rates)

**Must answer "yes/no" question on Form 1040**

flashcards.front: When is Cancellation of Debt (COD) income excludable?
flashcards.back:
**Exclusions (IRC §108):**
• Bankruptcy (to extent discharged)
• Insolvency (to extent insolvent)
• Qualified farm indebtedness
• Qualified real property business debt
• Qualified principal residence indebtedness

**Tax Attribute Reduction:**
Must reduce tax attributes for excluded amounts

**Form 1099-C issued for $600+**

flashcards.front: How is barter income reported?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:**
FMV of goods/services received is taxable income

**Reporting:**
• Form 1099-B (barter exchanges)
• Include in gross income
• Treated as cash transaction

**Basis:**
FMV of property received = Basis

**Example:** Trade accounting services for car repair - both sides have income equal to FMV received

flashcards.front: What is the Wash Sale Rule?
flashcards.back:
**Rule:** Cannot deduct loss if substantially identical security purchased within 30 days before OR after sale

**Period:** 61-day window (30 before + sale date + 30 after)

**Effect:**
• Deferred loss added to basis of replacement
• Holding period tacks

**Applies to:**
• Stocks, bonds, options
• Mutual funds of same company

flashcards.front: How are collectibles taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Maximum Rate:** 28% (instead of normal 0/15/20%)

**Collectibles Include:**
• Art, antiques
• Rugs, gems
• Metals (gold, silver)
• Stamps, coins
• Alcoholic beverages
• Some tangible personal property

**Holding period >1 year = 28% max rate**

**Short-term = Ordinary income rates**

flashcards.front: What is the Section 1244 stock loss benefit?
flashcards.back:
**Benefit:** Ordinary loss treatment (instead of capital)

**Limits:**
• $50,000/year ($100,000 MFJ)
• Excess is capital loss

**Requirements:**
• Domestic small business corporation
• Stock issued for money or property
• Corporation capital ≤ $1M at issuance
• Originally issued to taxpayer

**Benefit:** Ordinary loss = full AGI deduction

flashcards.front: How is investment interest expense deducted?
flashcards.back:
**Deduction Limit:**
Net investment income (interest, dividends, ST capital gains)

**Excess:** Carries forward indefinitely

**Election:** Treat qualified dividends/LTCG as investment income (forfeits preferential rates)

**NOT Investment Interest:**
• Home mortgage interest
• Passive activity interest
• Interest on tax-exempt investments

flashcards.front: What is the self-employed health insurance deduction?
flashcards.back:
**100% Above-the-Line Deduction for:**
• Health insurance premiums (medical, dental, vision)
• Long-term care premiums (per age limits)
• Coverage for self, spouse, dependents
• Children under 27

**Limit:**
Cannot exceed net self-employment income

**Cannot claim if eligible for subsidized employer plan**

flashcards.front: What are the Net Operating Loss (NOL) rules for individuals?
flashcards.back:
**Post-TCJA Rules:**
• No carryback (generally)
• Unlimited carryforward
• 80% of taxable income limit

**Farm NOL:** 2-year carryback allowed

**Casualty NOL:** Special rules

**Order:** Current year income first, then oldest NOL carryforward

**Form 172 for farming NOL carryback**

flashcards.front: What are the hobby loss rules?
flashcards.back:
**IRC §183 - Activities Not for Profit:**

**Presumption of Profit Motive:**
• 3 of 5 years profitable (general)
• 2 of 7 years (horse breeding/racing)

**9 Factors:** Intent to profit, expertise, time devoted, success in similar activities, etc.

**If Hobby:**
• Report income (gross)
• TCJA: No deduction for hobby expenses

**If Business:** Full deduction for expenses

flashcards.front: What are the At-Risk limitations?
flashcards.back:
**Deduction Limited to Amount At-Risk:**
• Cash contributed
• Adjusted basis of property contributed
• Amounts borrowed for which personally liable
• Qualified nonrecourse financing (real estate only)

**Suspended Losses:** Carry forward indefinitely

**Recapture:** If at-risk amount goes negative

**Form 6198 required**

flashcards.front: What are the Passive Activity Loss rules?
flashcards.back:
**Passive Activities:**
• Trade/business without material participation
• Rental activities (generally)

**Losses Limited to:**
Passive income only

**$25,000 Rental Exception:**
• Active participation
• Phases out $100K-$150K MAGI

**REP Exception:** 750+ hours, >50% services

flashcards.front: What are related party transaction rules for losses and sales?
flashcards.back:
**Loss Disallowance (§267):**
No deduction for loss on sale to related party

**Related Parties:**
• Family (spouse, siblings, ancestors, lineal descendants)
• >50% owned corporation/partnership
• Trusts/beneficiaries

**Installment Rule (§453):**
Gain recognized if resold within 2 years

**Capital Asset (§1239):**
Gain on depreciable property = Ordinary income

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 gross income filing thresholds?
flashcards.back:
**Single:** $14,600
**MFJ (both <65):** $29,200
**MFJ (one ≥65):** $30,750
**MFS:** $5
**HOH:** $21,900
**QSS:** $29,200

**Self-employed:** Must file if net SE income ≥ $400

**Dependents:** Earned income > $14,600 OR unearned income > $1,300

flashcards.front: How do you calculate the basis in property received in a §1031 like-kind exchange?
flashcards.back:
**Formula:**
Basis of property received =
(Basis of property given) - (Boot received) + (Gain recognized) + (Boot paid)

**OR:** FMV of new property - Deferred gain

**Boot = cash + FMV of non-like-kind property**

Gain recognized = Lesser of: (1) Gain realized or (2) Boot received
flashcards.formula: New Basis = Old Basis - Boot Received + Gain Recognized + Boot Paid

flashcards.front: How to remember the key differences between AOTC and Lifetime Learning Credit?
flashcards.back:
**AOTC = 4-A Rule:**
- First **4** years
- **$4**0% refundable
- Max **$2,500** (2+5-0.5=4-ish)
- **A**mericans only (citizenship req)

**LLC = Lifetime = LESS:**
- **L**ifetime availability
- **E**veryone eligible (no felony restriction)
- **S**maller amount (20% of $10K)
- **S**ingle or part-time OK

**AOTC stricter; LLC more flexible**
flashcards.mnemonic: AOTC = 4 A Rule; LLC = LESS restrictions

flashcards.front: What qualifies a taxpayer as a Real Estate Professional for passive activity purposes?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements (meet BOTH):**
1. More than 750 hours in real estate activities during the year
2. More than 50% of personal services in real estate

**Real estate activities include:**
- Development
- Construction
- Acquisition
- Management
- Leasing
- Brokerage

**Effect:** Rental real estate is NOT passive

**Each spouse tested separately (except MFJ election)**

flashcards.front: How is the estimated tax penalty calculated and what are the safe harbors?
flashcards.back:
**Penalty = Interest on underpayment**

**Safe Harbors (No penalty if paid):**
- 100% of prior year tax (110% if AGI >$150K)
- 90% of current year tax
- Balance due <$1,000

**Quarterly Due Dates:**
- Q1: April 15
- Q2: June 15
- Q3: Sept 15
- Q4: Jan 15

**Annualized income installment method available for uneven income**

flashcards.front: What is the Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) and when does it apply?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 3.8% surtax

**Applies when:**
- MAGI exceeds threshold
- Taxpayer has net investment income

**Thresholds:**
- MFJ/QSS: $250,000
- Single/HOH: $200,000
- MFS: $125,000

**Investment Income includes:**
- Interest, dividends, capital gains
- Rental income
- Royalties
- Passive activity income

**Tax = 3.8% × Lesser of: NII or (MAGI - threshold)**

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for Head of Household filing status?
flashcards.back:
1) Unmarried (or considered unmarried) on last day of year
2) Paid more than half the cost of keeping up a home
3) Qualifying person lived with you more than half the year

Exceptions: Dependent parent doesn\
flashcards.example: Single parent with custody of child qualifies if they pay majority of household costs

flashcards.front: What is Qualifying Surviving Spouse (formerly Qualifying Widow/er)?
flashcards.back: Filing status available for 2 years after spouse\
flashcards.example: Spouse died in 2024. Taxpayer can use QSS for 2025 and 2026 if requirements met.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of Gross Income?
flashcards.back:
All income from whatever source derived, including:
• Compensation for services
• Gross income from business
• Gains from property dealings
• Interest, dividends, rents, royalties
• Alimony (pre-2019 agreements)
• Annuities, pensions
• Income from discharge of indebtedness

flashcards.front: What is Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)?
flashcards.back:
Gross Income MINUS "above-the-line" deductions:
• Educator expenses ($300)
• HSA contributions
• Self-employment tax (1/2)
• Self-employed health insurance
• SEP/SIMPLE/qualified plan contributions
• Student loan interest ($2,500 max)
• IRA contributions
• Alimony paid (pre-2019)

flashcards.front: What is Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI)?
flashcards.back:
AGI with certain items added back. The add-backs vary by the tax benefit being calculated:

• Roth IRA: Add back traditional IRA deduction, student loan interest, foreign earned income exclusion
• Premium Tax Credit: Add back excluded foreign income, tax-exempt interest
• IRMAA (Medicare): Add back tax-exempt interest
flashcards.example: For Roth IRA eligibility: MAGI = AGI + traditional IRA deduction + student loan interest deduction

flashcards.front: What is the doctrine of Constructive Receipt?
flashcards.back:
Income is taxable when:
1) It is credited to your account
2) Set apart for you
3) Made available for you to draw upon
4) You could have received it

Even if you don\
flashcards.example: Dividend check arrives Dec 28 but you don\

flashcards.front: What is the difference between refundable and non-refundable credits?
flashcards.back:
**Refundable Credits:** Can reduce tax below zero = you get a refund
• EITC, ACTC, AOTC (40%), Premium Tax Credit

**Non-Refundable Credits:** Can only reduce tax to zero
• Child Tax Credit (non-refundable portion)
• Lifetime Learning Credit
• Child/Dependent Care Credit
• Saver\

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 Standard Deduction amounts?
flashcards.back:
**2024 Standard Deduction:**
• Single: $14,600
• MFJ/QSS: $29,200
• MFS: $14,600
• HOH: $21,900

**Additional for Age 65+ or Blind:**
• Single/HOH: $1,950 each
• MFJ/MFS/QSS: $1,550 each

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 IRA contribution limits?
flashcards.back:
**2024 IRA Contribution Limits:**
• Under 50: $7,000
• 50 or older: $8,000 (catch-up)

**Contribution Deadline:** April 15 of following year

**Deductibility phaseouts (if covered by employer plan):**
• Single: $77,000-$87,000
• MFJ (contributor covered): $123,000-$143,000
• MFJ (spouse covered): $230,000-$240,000

flashcards.front: What determines short-term vs long-term capital gains?
flashcards.back:
**Holding Period:**
• Short-term: 1 year or less
• Long-term: More than 1 year

**Tax Rates:**
• Short-term: Taxed as ordinary income
• Long-term: 0%, 15%, or 20% based on taxable income

**Start date:** Day after acquisition
**End date:** Day of sale (inclusive)
flashcards.example: Buy Jan 15, 2024. Must hold until Jan 16, 2025 for long-term treatment.

flashcards.front: What is the Section 121 Home Sale Exclusion?
flashcards.back:
**Exclusion Amount:**
• Single: $250,000
• MFJ: $500,000

**Requirements:**
• Owned home 2 of last 5 years
• Lived in home 2 of last 5 years (as primary residence)
• Haven\

flashcards.front: What is the Kiddie Tax and who does it apply to?
flashcards.back: **Kiddie Tax:** Unearned income of certain children is taxed at parent\

flashcards.front: What qualifies as a Qualified Dividend?
flashcards.back:
**Qualified Dividends** are taxed at long-term capital gains rates (0%, 15%, 20%)

**Requirements:**
1) Paid by U.S. corporation or qualified foreign corporation
2) Not specifically excluded (REITs, MLPs, etc.)
3) Meet holding period: 61 days during 121-day period around ex-dividend date

**Non-qualified dividends** are taxed as ordinary income.

flashcards.front: What is the Qualified Business Income (QBI) Deduction?
flashcards.back:
**Section 199A Deduction:**
• Up to 20% of qualified business income
• From pass-through entities (sole proprietorship, partnership, S corp)

**Limitations:**
• Taxable income threshold: $191,950 (S) / $383,900 (MFJ)
• Above threshold: W-2 wage/property basis limits apply
• Specified service businesses phase out above threshold

**Sunsets after 2025** unless extended

flashcards.front: What is the Child Tax Credit for 2024?
flashcards.back:
**2024 Child Tax Credit:**
• $2,000 per qualifying child under 17
• Up to $1,700 refundable (ACTC)

**Phaseout:**
• Begins at $200,000 (S) / $400,000 (MFJ)
• Reduces $50 per $1,000 over threshold

**Qualifying child requirements:**
• Under 17 at year end
• Your dependent, lived with you 6+ months
• Valid SSN

flashcards.front: What are the basic requirements for the Earned Income Credit (EIC)?
flashcards.back:
**EIC Requirements:**
1) Have earned income (wages, SE income)
2) AGI and earned income below limits
3) Valid SSN for you, spouse, qualifying children
4) Not MFS (with limited exception)
5) Be U.S. citizen or resident alien all year
6) Investment income ≤ $11,600 (2024)

**Maximum credits vary by # of qualifying children (0-3+)**

flashcards.front: What is the difference between AOTC and Lifetime Learning Credit?
flashcards.back:
**American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC):**
• Up to $2,500/student (40% refundable)
• First 4 years of college only
• At least half-time enrollment
• No felony drug conviction

**Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC):**
• Up to $2,000 per return (non-refundable)
• Any post-secondary education
• Any number of years
• No enrollment minimum

flashcards.front: How is Social Security taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Up to 85% of Social Security benefits may be taxable**

Based on "provisional income" = AGI + tax-exempt interest + 50% of SS benefits

**Single filers:**
• Below $25,000: Not taxable
• $25,000-$34,000: Up to 50% taxable
• Above $34,000: Up to 85% taxable

**MFJ:**
• Below $32,000: Not taxable
• $32,000-$44,000: Up to 50% taxable
• Above $44,000: Up to 85% taxable

flashcards.front: When must Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) begin?
flashcards.back:
**RMD Start Age (SECURE 2.0):**
• Born 1951-1959: Age 73
• Born 1960+: Age 75

**First RMD deadline:** April 1 of year after reaching RMD age (but 2 RMDs in that year!)

**Subsequent RMDs:** December 31 each year

**Penalty for missing RMD:** 25% of amount not withdrawn (reduced to 10% if corrected timely)

flashcards.front: What are the key rules for Roth IRA contributions and distributions?
flashcards.back:
**Roth IRA Contributions:**
• Same limits as Traditional ($7,000/$8,000)
• Income phaseouts: $146,000-$161,000 (S) / $230,000-$240,000 (MFJ)
• No age limit for contributions

**Qualified Distributions (tax-free):**
• Account open 5+ years AND
• Age 59½, death, disability, or first home ($10K max)

**No RMDs required for original owner**

flashcards.front: Single Filing Status Requirements
flashcards.back: Use if on the last day of the year you are unmarried or legally separated (by divorce or separate maintenance decree) and do not qualify for another filing status.

flashcards.front: Married Filing Jointly (MFJ) Requirements
flashcards.back: Married couples can file a joint return. If a spouse dies during the year, the surviving spouse can file MFJ for that year unless they remarry before year-end.

flashcards.front: Married Filing Separately (MFS) Disadvantages
flashcards.back: Generally higher tax rates; cannot take credits like EITC, Child and Dependent Care (in most cases), Education credits; standard deduction must be same for both (if one details, both must)

flashcards.front: Head of Household (HOH) Requirements
flashcards.back: 1. Unmarried or "considered unmarried" on last day of year\n2. Paid >50% cost of keeping up a home\n3. Qualifying person lived with you >50% of year (exception for dependent parents who don\

flashcards.front: Qualifying Surviving Spouse (QSS) Requirements
flashcards.back: Can file as QSS for 2 years following spouse\

flashcards.front: Standard Deduction - Single (2024)
Standard Deduction - MFJ (2024)
Standard Deduction - HOH (2024)
Additional Standard Deduction (65+ or Blind)
flashcards.back: Unmarried: +$1,950\nMarried: +$1,550 per qualifying person

flashcards.front: Child Tax Credit (CTC) Amount (2024)
flashcards.back: $2,000 per qualifying child under age 17

flashcards.front: CTC Refundable Portion (ACTC) Limit (2024)
flashcards.back: Up to $1,700 per child is refundable

flashcards.front: Other Dependent Credit (ODC)
flashcards.back: $500 non-refundable credit for dependents who don"t qualify for CTC (e.g., child 17+, parents)

flashcards.front: Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) Eligibility Basic Rules
flashcards.back:
1. Valid SSN for self and dependents
2. US Citizen or Resident Alien
3. Not filing MFS (exceptions apply)
4. Investment income < $11,600 (2024)
5. Earned income

flashcards.front: EITC Disqualified Income
flashcards.back: Investment income over threshold ($11,600 for 2024) disqualifies you. Includes interest, dividends, cap gains, royalties, rental income.

flashcards.front: American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) Scope
flashcards.back: First 4 years of post-secondary education. 100% of first $2,000 + 25% of next $2,000. Max $2,500.

flashcards.front: Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC) Scope
flashcards.back: Any post-secondary or skill-improvement courses. 20% of first $10,000 expenses. Max $2,000 per return (not per student).

flashcards.front: AOTC vs LLC: Refundability
flashcards.back: AOTC: Up to 40% ($1,000) refundable.\nLLC: Non-refundable.

flashcards.front: Short-Term Capital Gains Rate
flashcards.back: Taxed as ordinary income (marginal tax brackets)

flashcards.front: Long-Term Capital Gains Rates
flashcards.back: 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on taxable income

flashcards.front: Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)
flashcards.back: 3.8% additional tax on lesser of NII or MAGI excess over threshold ($200k Single, $250k MFJ)

flashcards.front: Capital Loss Limits
flashcards.back: Can offset capital gains. Excess loss deduction limited to $3,000 ($1,500 MFS) against ordinary income. Unused carries forward indefinitely.

flashcards.front: Section 121 Exclusion Amount
flashcards.back: $250,000 (Single) / $500,000 (MFJ) of gain excluded from income

flashcards.front: Section 121 Eligibility Test
flashcards.back: Ownership and Use: Owned and lived in home as main home for at least 2 of last 5 years.

flashcards.front: Qualifying Child Tests
flashcards.back: Relationship, Age, Residence (>50%), Support (child didn"t provide >50%), Joint Return (none)

flashcards.front: Qualifying Relative Tests
flashcards.back: Not a Qualifying Child, Member of Household OR Relationship, Gross Income (<$5,050 for 2024), Support (taxpayer provided >50%)

flashcards.front: Constructive Receipt
flashcards.back: Income is taxed when it is made available to you without substantial restriction, even if you don"t take possession.

flashcards.front: RMD Age (SECURE 2.0)
flashcards.back: Age 73 (starting 2023)

flashcards.front: Early Withdrawal Penalty Exceptions
flashcards.back: Death, Disability, Medical expenses (>7.5% AGI), 72(t) payments, Higher Education, First-time homebuyer ($10k)

flashcards.front: Roth IRA Contribution Limit (2024)
State and Local Tax (SALT) Limit
flashcards.back: Capped at $10,000 ($5,000 MFS) total for property and income (or sales) taxes.

flashcards.front: Medical Expense Threshold
flashcards.back: Deductible only to the extent they exceed 7.5% of AGI.

flashcards.front: Charitable Contribution Limit (Cash)
flashcards.back: Generally 60% of AGI.

flashcards.front: Safe Harbor Rules for Underpayment Penalty
flashcards.back: No penalty if you pay: 90% of current year tax OR 100% of prior year tax (110% if AGI > $150k)

flashcards.front: Kiddie Tax Applicability
flashcards.back: Applies to children under 18 (or student under 24) with unearned income > $2,600 (2024). Taxed at parent"s rate.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for calculating taxable income?
flashcards.back:
Gross Income
  − Above-the-line deductions
= **Adjusted Gross Income (AGI)**
  − Greater of (Standard OR Itemized Deductions)
  − QBI Deduction (if applicable)
= **Taxable Income**
flashcards.formula: Taxable Income = GI - Above-line - (Std or Itemized) - QBI

flashcards.front: How do you calculate Self-Employment Tax?
flashcards.back:
**Step 1:** Net SE Income × 92.35% = SE Tax Base

**Step 2:** SE Tax Base × 15.3% = SE Tax
(12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare)

**Note:** SS wage base for 2024: $168,600
Additional Medicare: 0.9% on earned income > $200K (S) / $250K (MFJ)

flashcards.front: What is the above-the-line deduction for Self-Employment Tax?
flashcards.back:
**Deduction = 50% of Self-Employment Tax**

This is an adjustment to income (above-the-line), reducing AGI.

Purpose: Mirrors the employer portion of FICA that employees don\

flashcards.front: How do you calculate capital gain or loss on sale of property?
flashcards.back:
**Amount Realized**
  (Sale price − selling expenses)
− **Adjusted Basis**
  (Cost + improvements − depreciation)
= **Gain or Loss**

If AR > AB = Gain
If AR < AB = Loss

flashcards.front: How do you calculate Net Capital Gain for tax purposes?
flashcards.back:
**Step 1:** Net ST gains and losses together
**Step 2:** Net LT gains and losses together
**Step 3:** Net the two results

**Net Capital Gain formula:**
Net LT Capital Gain − Net ST Capital Loss

**Capital Loss Limit:** $3,000/year deductible against ordinary income, excess carries forward
flashcards.formula: Net Capital Gain = NLTCG − NSTCL (if positive)

flashcards.front: What is the Straight-Line Depreciation formula?
flashcards.back:
**Annual Depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life**

For tax purposes (MACRS), salvage value is $0.

**Half-Year Convention:** First and last year get half depreciation
**Mid-Month Convention:** Real property - depreciation starts mid-month of placed in service

flashcards.front: How is the early withdrawal penalty calculated for retirement accounts?
flashcards.back:
**Penalty = Taxable Distribution × 10%**

Applies to distributions before age 59½ from:
• IRAs (Traditional and Roth earnings)
• 401(k), 403(b), etc.

**Exceptions:** Death, disability, SEPP, medical expenses > 7.5% AGI, first home ($10K IRA), education, health insurance while unemployed

flashcards.front: How is the American Opportunity Tax Credit calculated?
flashcards.back:
**AOTC = 100% of first $2,000 + 25% of next $2,000**

Maximum credit: $2,500
Refundable portion: 40% ($1,000 max)

**Phaseout:**
• Single: $80,000-$90,000
• MFJ: $160,000-$180,000

flashcards.front: How are medical expense deductions calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Deductible Medical Expenses = Total Medical − (7.5% × AGI)**

Only the amount EXCEEDING 7.5% of AGI is deductible.

**Qualified expenses:** Doctors, dentists, prescriptions, medical equipment, insurance premiums (not pretax), mileage for medical, long-term care (limits apply)

flashcards.front: How is the personal casualty loss deduction calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Currently limited to federally declared disasters**

**Formula:**
Loss = Lesser of (Decline in FMV OR Adjusted Basis)
  − Insurance/reimbursement
  − $100 per event
  − 10% of AGI

**For 2018-2025:** Only federally declared disaster losses are deductible
flashcards.formula: Net Loss = (Lesser of ΔFMV or Basis) − Insurance − $100 − (10% × AGI)

flashcards.front: What are the safe harbor rules for estimated tax payments?
flashcards.back:
**No Penalty if payments ≥:**
• 90% of current year tax, OR
• 100% of prior year tax (110% if prior year AGI > $150,000)

**Due Dates:** April 15, June 15, Sept 15, Jan 15

**Annualized Income Method:** Alternative if income uneven throughout year
flashcards.formula: Safe Harbor = Greater of (90% current) or (100%/110% prior)

flashcards.front: How is the Child and Dependent Care Credit calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Credit = Qualifying Expenses × Credit Percentage**

**Expense Limit:**
• 1 qualifying person: $3,000
• 2+ qualifying persons: $6,000

**Credit Percentage:** 20%-35% based on AGI
• 35% for AGI ≤ $15,000
• Declines 1% per $2,000 to 20% at $43,000+

**Must have earned income** (expenses limited to lower-earning spouse)
flashcards.formula: Credit = Min(Expenses, Limit, Earned Income) × Rate%

flashcards.front: What is the basis of property received as a gift?
flashcards.back: **General Rule:** Carryover basis (donor\
flashcards.example: Donor basis $10K, FMV at gift $7K, sold for $8K → No gain/loss

flashcards.front: What is the basis of property received by inheritance?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:** Stepped-up (or down) basis to FMV at date of death

**Alternative Valuation Date:** 6 months after death (if reduces estate value)

**Community Property:** Both halves get step-up

**Exception:** Income in Respect of Decedent (IRD) - no step-up
flashcards.formula: Inherited Basis = FMV at Death (or AVD)

flashcards.front: How do you calculate Net Earnings from Self-Employment?
flashcards.back:
**Net SE Income = Gross SE Income − SE Business Expenses**

Includes:
• Schedule C net profit
• Partner\
flashcards.formula: Net SE Income = Business Revenue − Business Expenses

flashcards.front: SENSE - When must you file regardless of income?
flashcards.back:
**S**elf-employment income ≥ $400
**E**mployer advance of EIC received
**N**et church employee earnings ≥ $108.28
**S**pecial taxes owed (AMT, household, tips, etc.)
**E**xcess HSA/Archer MSA contributions

flashcards.front: CARES - What are the tests for a Qualifying Child?
flashcards.back:
**C**lose relative (child, stepchild, sibling, etc.)
**A**ge (under 19, or 24 if student, any age if disabled)
**R**esidency (lived with you 6+ months)
**E**liminate joint return (with spouse)
**S**upport - child did NOT provide >50% of own support

flashcards.front: SUIRN - What are the tests for a Qualifying Relative?
flashcards.back:
**S**upport - you provided >50%
**U**nder gross income limit (<$5,050 in 2024)
**I**t"s not a qualifying child of anyone
**R**elative (or member of household all year)
**N**ot joint return (with spouse claiming refund)

flashcards.front: GIDM - What types of receipts are excluded from gross income?
flashcards.back:
**G**ifts and inheritances
**I**njury/sickness payments (physical)
**D**eath benefits (life insurance)
**M**unicipal bond interest
flashcards.example: Life insurance proceeds, workers" comp for injury, municipal bond interest - all excluded

flashcards.front: DENIM - What employer-provided fringe benefits are excluded from income?
flashcards.back:
**D**e minimis benefits (small value)
**E**mployee discounts (qualified)
**N**o-additional-cost services
**I**nsurance (health, life up to $50K, disability premiums)
**M**eals & lodging (on premises, for employer convenience)

flashcards.front: MMITCH - What are the main categories of itemized deductions?
flashcards.back:
**M**edical expenses (> 7.5% AGI)
**M**ortgage interest ($750K limit)
**I**nvestment interest (to extent of investment income)
**T**axes (SALT capped at $10K)
**C**haritable contributions
**H**arm/casualty losses (federally declared disasters only)

flashcards.front: SHITE MAPS - What are the main above-the-line deductions?
flashcards.back:
**S**elf-employment tax (50%)
**H**ealth insurance (self-employed)
**I**RA contributions
**T**eacher expenses ($300)
**E**arly withdrawal penalty

**M**oving expenses (military only)
**A**limony paid (pre-2019 agreements)
**P**ension/SEP/SIMPLE contributions
**S**tudent loan interest ($2,500 max)

flashcards.front: QID-45-180 - What are the 1031 Like-Kind Exchange rules?
flashcards.back:
**Q**ualified intermediary required
**I**dentification period: 45 days
**D**eed must transfer within 180 days

**Rules:**
• 45 days to identify replacement property
• 180 days to complete exchange
• Like-kind = real property for real property
• Only investment/business property (not personal)

flashcards.front: SEPP DIME HQ - What are the exceptions to the 10% early withdrawal penalty?
flashcards.back:
**S**EPP (Substantially Equal Periodic Payments)
**E**ducation expenses (IRA only)
**P**ermanent disability
**P**urchase first home ($10K, IRA only)

**D**eath
**I**RS levy
**M**edical expenses > 7.5% AGI
**E**mployee separation (age 55+ from 401k)

**H**ealth insurance (while unemployed, IRA)
**Q**ualified reservist distributions

flashcards.front: CAPE - What are the main refundable tax credits?
flashcards.back:
**C**hild Tax Credit (refundable portion - ACTC)
**A**OTC (40% refundable)
**P**remium Tax Credit
**E**arned Income Credit
flashcards.example: These credits can result in a refund even if you owe no tax

flashcards.front: FIFI - What disqualifies someone from the Earned Income Credit?
flashcards.back:
**F**iling MFS (with exceptions)
**I**nvestment income > $11,600 (2024)
**F**oreign earned income excluded
**I**nvalid SSN (for you/spouse/child)

Also: Non-resident alien (unless filing jointly with U.S. citizen/resident)

flashcards.front: ID CAN - What adjusts the basis of property?
flashcards.back:
**I**mprovements (add to basis)
**D**epreciation (reduces basis)

**C**asualty losses (reduces basis)
**A**ssessments for improvements (adds to basis)
**N**on-deductible legal fees for title (adds to basis)

flashcards.front: SLICE - What tax benefits are lost when filing MFS?
flashcards.back:
**S**tudent loan interest deduction
**L**ifetime Learning Credit
**I**ncome exclusion for EE bonds
**C**hild/Dependent Care Credit (limited)
**E**IC (Earned Income Credit)

Also: AOTC, adoption credit, and many others are reduced or eliminated

flashcards.front: BFR - What factors distinguish employees from independent contractors?
flashcards.back:
**B**ehavioral Control
• Who controls how work is done?
• Training provided?

**F**inancial Control
• Significant investment?
• Unreimbursed expenses?
• Profit/loss opportunity?

**R**elationship Type
• Written contract?
• Employee benefits?
• Permanency of relationship?

flashcards.front: 60-30-20 - What are the charitable contribution AGI limits?
flashcards.back:
**60% of AGI** - Cash to public charities
**30% of AGI** - Capital gain property to public charities, OR cash to private foundations
**20% of AGI** - Capital gain property to private foundations

**5-Year Carryforward** for excess contributions

**Note:** Cash limit was temporarily 100% (2020-2021), returned to 60%

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for a tax-free Section 351 transfer?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Transfer property to corporation
• Solely in exchange for stock
• Transferors control corporation immediately after (80%+ of each class)

**Control:** 80% voting + 80% nonvoting

**Gain Recognition:**
• Boot received (cash/property)
• Liabilities exceed basis
• Services rendered

**Basis:** Carryover from transferor

flashcards.front: What are the rules for tax-free partnership formation?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule (§721):**
No gain/loss on contribution of property for partnership interest

**Exceptions:**
• Investment partnerships (diversification)
• Liabilities shifted in excess of basis
• Disguised sales (§707)

**Basis Rules:**
• Partner: Carryover basis in interest
• Partnership: Carryover basis in property

**Holding period tacks for capital/1231 assets**

flashcards.front: How is a partner\
flashcards.back:
**Initial Basis:**
• Cash contributed
• Adjusted basis of property contributed
• + Share of partnership liabilities

**Adjustments:**
• + Share of income
• + Additional contributions
• - Distributions
• - Share of losses
• - Share of nondeductible expenses

**Cannot go below zero (losses suspended)**
flashcards.formula: Basis = Contributions + Income Share - Distributions - Loss Share

flashcards.front: What is the corporate income tax rate?
flashcards.back:
**Flat Tax Rate:** 21% (post-TCJA)

**Prior Law:** Graduated rates 15%-35%

**Key Points:**
• No 0% bracket
• No preferential capital gains rate
• Net capital losses: No deduction (carryback 3 / forward 5)
• Fiscal year allowed

**Personal Service Corporations:**
Flat 21% (PSC status still relevant for other rules)

flashcards.front: What is the Accumulated Earnings Tax?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 20% of accumulated taxable income

**Purpose:** Penalty for unreasonable accumulation to avoid shareholder dividends

**Accumulated Earnings Credit:**
• $250,000 general ($150,000 PSC)
• Or business needs, if greater

**Reasonable Needs:**
• Expansion, equipment
• Debt retirement
• Working capital needs

**Burden:** IRS must prove unreasonable accumulation

flashcards.front: What is the Personal Holding Company (PHC) tax?
flashcards.back:
**Rate:** 20% of undistributed PHC income

**PHC Status (Both Tests):**
• 5 or fewer individuals own >50%
• 60%+ of income is PHC income

**PHC Income:**
• Dividends, interest, royalties
• Rents (if <50% income)
• Personal service contracts

**Avoid by:** Dividend distributions

flashcards.front: What is the Dividends Received Deduction (DRD)?
flashcards.back:
**Deduction Percentages:**
• <20% ownership: 50% DRD
• 20-79% ownership: 65% DRD
• 80%+ ownership: 100% DRD

**Taxable Income Limit:**
Cannot create/increase NOL (65%/50% limited)

**Requirements:**
• Dividends from domestic corporations
• Stock held 46+ days

**100% DRD: No taxable income limit**

flashcards.front: What are the corporate charitable contribution rules?
flashcards.back:
**Limit:** 10% of taxable income (before DRD, NOL, charitable deduction)

**Carryover:** 5 years

**Enhanced Deduction:**
• Inventory: Cost + 50% unrealized appreciation (capped)
• Scientific research property: Similar

**Timing:** Accrual-basis can deduct if paid by 3½ months after year-end with board authorization

flashcards.front: What are the eligibility requirements for S corporation status?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Domestic corporation
• ≤100 shareholders
• One class of stock
• Only eligible shareholders

**Eligible Shareholders:**
• Individuals
• Estates
• Certain trusts
• Tax-exempt organizations (501(c)(3))

**Ineligible:**
• Partnerships, corporations
• Nonresident aliens

flashcards.front: What is the S corporation Built-In Gains (BIG) tax?
flashcards.back:
**Applies when:**
• C corp converts to S corp
• Sells appreciated assets within recognition period

**Recognition Period:** 5 years

**Tax Rate:** 21% (corporate rate)

**Net Recognized Built-In Gain:**
Lesser of recognized gains or built-in gain at conversion

**Passes through to shareholders (net of tax)**

flashcards.front: How is S corporation shareholder stock basis calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Initial Basis:**
Cost or basis received in transfer

**Increases:**
• Additional contributions
• Share of income (pro-rata)
• Share of separately stated items

**Decreases (in order):**
1. Distributions
2. Non-deductible expenses
3. Deductions and losses

**Cannot go below zero**

flashcards.front: What are the loss limitation rules for S corporation shareholders?
flashcards.back:
**Order of Limitations:**
1. Tax basis (stock + debt basis)
2. At-risk (IRC §465)
3. Passive activity (IRC §469)
4. Excess business loss (IRC §461(l))

**Debt Basis:**
• Direct loans from shareholder only
• NOT guarantees
• NOT entity-level debt

**Carryforward:** Indefinitely

flashcards.front: What is the Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA)?
flashcards.back:
**Purpose:** Tracks S corp earnings that can be distributed tax-free

**Similar to:** Shareholder basis (but corporate level)

**Adjustments:**
• + Income
• - Distributions
• - Losses
• - Nondeductible expenses

**Can go negative** (except for distributions)

**Important for:** C corp conversion or AE&P

flashcards.front: What items must be separately stated on Schedule K-1?
flashcards.back:
**Separately Stated:**
• Capital gains/losses
• Section 1231 gains/losses
• Charitable contributions
• Section 179 expense
• Investment interest expense
• Rental income/loss
• Foreign taxes
• Tax-exempt income
• Credits

**Reason:** Items with special character or limitations at partner level

flashcards.front: What are guaranteed payments to partners?
flashcards.back:
**Definition:** Payments for services or capital use, determined without regard to partnership income

**Treatment - Partner:**
• Ordinary income
• Subject to SE tax (services)
• NOT subject to withholding

**Treatment - Partnership:**
• Deductible (like wages)
• Reduces ordinary income

**Reported on Schedule K-1 separately**

flashcards.front: How are partnership distributions taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Current Distributions:**
• Generally tax-free
• Reduce partner basis
• Gain only if cash > basis

**Liquidating Distributions:**
• Gain: Cash > basis
• Loss: Only if cash + inventory + unrealized receivables

**Basis of Property:**
• Carryover (current)
• Substituted (liquidating)

**Hot assets may trigger ordinary income**

flashcards.front: What are partnership "hot assets"?
flashcards.back:
**IRC §751 Hot Assets:**
• Unrealized receivables (including depreciation recapture)
• Inventory items
• Substantially appreciated inventory

**Effect:**
• Sale of partnership interest: Ordinary income
• Disproportionate distributions: Ordinary income

**Purpose:** Prevent converting ordinary income to capital gain

**Substantially appreciated:** FMV > 120% of basis

flashcards.front: What requirements must special allocations meet?
flashcards.back:
**Substantial Economic Effect:**

**Economic Effect:**
• Allocations reflected in capital accounts
• Liquidating distributions per capital accounts
• Deficit restoration obligation

**Substantiality:**
• Reasonable possibility of affecting dollar amounts
• Not merely tax shifting

**Default:** Allocate per interests in profits

flashcards.front: What are the MACRS recovery period classes?
flashcards.back:
**Recovery Periods:**
• 3-year: Qualified rent-to-own, some horses
• 5-year: Autos, computers, office equipment
• 7-year: Office furniture, fixtures, most equipment
• 15-year: Land improvements, QIP
• 27.5 years: Residential rental
• 39 years: Nonresidential real property

**Methods:**
• Personal property: 200% DB
• Real property: Straight-line

flashcards.front: What are the Section 179 expense election limits (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Maximum Deduction:** $1,220,000

**Phase-out Threshold:** $3,050,000
(Dollar-for-dollar reduction)

**Eligible Property:**
• Tangible personal property
• Off-the-shelf software
• Qualified improvement property
• Certain real property improvements

**Limitations:**
• Taxable income limit (can carry forward)
• Specific vehicle limits

flashcards.front: What are the bonus depreciation rules (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Percentage:** 60% (2024)

**Phase-down Schedule:**
• 2022: 100%
• 2023: 80%
• 2024: 60%
• 2025: 40%
• 2026: 20%
• 2027+: 0%

**Eligible Property:**
• New or used (TCJA)
• Recovery period ≤20 years

**Elect Out:** By class, per year

flashcards.front: What are the listed property rules?
flashcards.back:
**Listed Property:**
• Passenger automobiles
• Property used for entertainment/recreation
• Computers (unless regular business establishment)

**Requirements:**
• Must be used >50% for business
• If not: ADS depreciation required

**Automobile Limits (2024):**
• Year 1: $12,200 (no bonus)
• Year 1: $20,200 (with bonus)
• Subsequent years limited

flashcards.front: What are Section 197 intangible assets?
flashcards.back:
**15-Year Amortization:**
• Goodwill
• Going concern value
• Workforce in place
• Customer lists
• Covenants not to compete
• Franchises, trademarks, trade names
• Patents and copyrights (if acquired with business)

**Straight-line method**

**Anti-churning rules apply to related party transactions**

flashcards.front: How are business start-up and organizational costs treated?
flashcards.back:
**Start-Up Costs (§195):**
• Deduct up to $5,000 (reduced if >$50K)
• Amortize remainder over 180 months

**Organizational Costs (§248):**
• Same: $5,000 deduction, 180-month amortization

**Election:** Made on first return

**Ineligible:** Expenses not meeting ordinary/necessary test

flashcards.front: What is the Section 163(j) business interest limitation?
flashcards.back:
**General Limit:**
Business interest expense ≤ 30% of ATI (adjusted taxable income)

**ATI:** Taxable income + interest expense + depreciation (certain years) - interest income

**Exceptions:**
• Small business ($29M gross receipts test)
• Electing real property/farming

**Carryforward:** Indefinite

flashcards.front: What are the deduction rules for business meals and entertainment?
flashcards.back:
**Meals (ordinary and necessary):**
• 50% deductible (general)
• 100% employer-operated cafeteria
• 100% de minimis food

**Entertainment:**
• 0% deductible (post-TCJA)
• Club dues: 0%

**Documentation:**
• Amount, date, place
• Business purpose
• Business relationship

flashcards.front: What are the business vs. nonbusiness bad debt rules?
flashcards.back:
**Business Bad Debt:**
• Ordinary deduction
• Partial worthlessness allowed
• Arise from trade or business

**Non-Business Bad Debt:**
• Short-term capital loss
• Only when totally worthless
• No partial deduction

**Accrual Method:** Must have included in income
**Cash Method:** No deduction (never in income)

flashcards.front: What are the NOL rules for businesses?
flashcards.back:
**Post-TCJA Rules:**
• No carryback (generally)
• Unlimited carryforward
• 80% of taxable income limit

**Exceptions:**
• Farming: 2-year carryback
• Certain disaster losses

**Order:** Current year income, then oldest NOL

**Pre-2018 NOLs:** 20-year carryforward, no 80% limit

flashcards.front: What are the FICA tax rates and wage bases (2024)?
flashcards.back:
**Social Security:**
• Rate: 6.2% employer + 6.2% employee
• Wage base: $168,600

**Medicare:**
• Rate: 1.45% employer + 1.45% employee
• No wage limit

**Additional Medicare:**
• 0.9% employee only
• Over $200K ($250K MFJ)

**FUTA:** 6.0% on first $7,000 (5.4% credit)

flashcards.front: What are the employment tax deposit rules?
flashcards.back:
**Monthly Depositor:**
• Prior year liability ≤ $50,000
• Due by 15th of following month

**Semi-Weekly Depositor:**
• Prior year liability > $50,000
• Wed-Fri payroll: Due Wednesday
• Sat-Tue payroll: Due Friday

**$100,000 Rule:**
• Accumulate $100K+ in one day
• Due next business day
• Become semi-weekly

flashcards.front: What is Form 941 and when is it due?
flashcards.back: **Form 941:** Employer\

flashcards.front: What is the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty?
flashcards.back:
**100% Penalty** on trust fund taxes not paid

**Trust Fund Taxes:**
• Employee income tax withholding
• Employee share of FICA

**NOT Trust Fund:**
• Employer share of FICA
• FUTA

**Personal Liability:**
Responsible persons who willfully fail to collect/pay

**Form 4180:** Interview to determine responsibility

flashcards.front: What is the General Business Credit (GBC)?
flashcards.back:
**Components Include:**
• Investment credit
• Work opportunity credit
• Research credit
• Disabled access credit
• Empowerment zone credit
• Small employer health credit

**Limitation:**
• Net income tax - Greater of TMT or 25% of net regular tax above $25K

**Carryback:** 1 year
**Carryforward:** 20 years

flashcards.front: What is the Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC)?
flashcards.back:
**Credit:** Generally 40% of first-year wages (up to $6,000)

**Target Groups:**
• Qualified veterans
• SNAP recipients
• Ex-felons
• Designated community residents
• SSI recipients
• Long-term unemployed

**Requirement:**
• 120+ hours worked (25% credit)
• 400+ hours (40% credit)

**Form 8850 pre-certification required**

flashcards.front: What is the Research and Development Tax Credit?
flashcards.back:
**Regular Credit:** 20% of excess QREs over base amount

**Alternative Simplified:** 14% of QREs over 50% of prior 3-year average

**Qualified Research Expenses:**
• Wages for qualified services
• Supplies
• Contract research (65%)

**Small Business:** Can offset AMT and payroll tax (up to $500K)

flashcards.front: What is the Rehabilitation Tax Credit?
flashcards.back:
**Credit Rate:** 20% of qualified rehabilitation expenditures

**Requirements:**
• Certified historic structure
• Substantial rehabilitation (> adjusted basis or $5K)
• Placed in service after work complete

**Recapture:** Ratably over 5 years if disposed

**Pre-1936 buildings:** No longer eligible (TCJA)

flashcards.front: What are the key differences between cash and accrual accounting methods?
flashcards.back:
**Cash Method:**
• Income when received
• Deductions when paid
• Simpler
• Limited for certain businesses

**Accrual Method:**
• Income when earned (all events + economic performance)
• Deductions when incurred
• Required for: Inventory businesses (exceptions), C corps >$29M

**Hybrid methods allowed**

flashcards.front: What is the doctrine of constructive receipt?
flashcards.back:
**Income Received When:**
• Credited to account
• Set apart
• Made available
• No substantial limitation

**Examples:**
• Check mailed in December (received if available)
• Matured bond interest
• Dividends available for pickup

**NOT Constructive:**
• Income subject to substantial restriction
• Merely earned but not available

flashcards.front: How do you change an accounting method?
flashcards.back:
**Form 3115:** Application for Change in Accounting Method

**Types:**
• Automatic changes: File with return
• Non-automatic: File in advance with IRS

**481(a) Adjustment:**
• Catch-up adjustment for cumulative difference
• Positive: 4-year spread (general)
• Negative: Full year 1

**IRS consent required**

flashcards.front: How is Cost of Goods Sold calculated?
flashcards.back:
**COGS = Beginning Inventory + Purchases - Ending Inventory**

**Include in Inventory:**
• Raw materials
• Work in process
• Finished goods

**Costs Include:**
• Direct materials
• Direct labor
• Factory overhead (capitalized)

**UNICAP (§263A) applies to manufacturers/resellers**

flashcards.front: What are the differences between FIFO and LIFO inventory methods?
flashcards.back:
**FIFO (First-In, First-Out):**
• Oldest costs in COGS
• Higher income in inflation
• Lower COGS
• Financial statement conformity NOT required

**LIFO (Last-In, First-Out):**
• Newest costs in COGS
• Lower income in inflation
• Higher COGS
• MUST use for financial statements (conformity rule)

**Cannot switch without consent**

flashcards.front: What are the Uniform Capitalization (UNICAP) rules?
flashcards.back:
**IRC §263A Requires:**
Capitalize direct costs + allocable indirect costs to inventory/self-constructed property

**Applies to:**
• Manufacturers
• Retailers (>$29M gross receipts)
• Property produced for use

**Costs Capitalized:**
• Direct materials, labor
• Factory overhead
• Purchasing, handling, storage

**Small taxpayer exception: $29M test**

flashcards.front: What is Section 1231 property and how is it taxed?
flashcards.back:
**Section 1231 Property:**
• Depreciable property used in trade/business
• Real property used in trade/business
• Held > 1 year
• NOT inventory

**Tax Treatment:**
• Net gains: Long-term capital gain
• Net losses: Ordinary loss

**Lookback Rule:** Prior 5-year §1231 losses recaptured as ordinary

flashcards.front: What is Section 1245 depreciation recapture?
flashcards.back:
**Applies to:** Personal property (equipment, machinery)

**Rule:** Lesser of:
• Gain recognized, OR
• Depreciation allowed/allowable

**Treatment:** Ordinary income

**Remainder:** Section 1231 gain

**Full recapture** of all depreciation as ordinary income

flashcards.front: What is Section 1250 depreciation recapture?
flashcards.back:
**Applies to:** Real property (buildings)

**General Rule:** Recapture excess depreciation over straight-line

**Unrecaptured §1250 Gain:**
• Straight-line depreciation on real property
• Maximum 25% rate

**Treatment of gain:**
1. Ordinary (if excess depreciation)
2. 25% unrecaptured §1250
3. Long-term capital gain

flashcards.front: What are the business installment sale rules?
flashcards.back:
**Ineligible for Installment:**
• Inventory
• Stocks/securities (publicly traded)
• Depreciation recapture (immediate recognition)
• Related party sales (restrictions)

**Gross Profit Ratio:**
(Sales Price - Basis) ÷ Sales Price

**Each payment:**
Principal × GP Ratio = Gain

**Interest must be stated or imputed**

flashcards.front: How are corporate distributions taxed to shareholders?
flashcards.back:
**Distribution Ordering:**
1. Dividend (to extent of E&P)
2. Return of capital (reduce stock basis)
3. Capital gain (after basis reduced to zero)

**Qualified Dividends:**
• 0%/15%/20% rates
• Must hold stock 61+ days

**Holding Period:** 121 days around ex-dividend

flashcards.front: How is corporate Earnings and Profits (E&P) calculated?
flashcards.back:
**E&P = Taxable Income ± Adjustments**

**Add Back:**
• Tax-exempt income
• Federal income tax refunds
• DRD

**Subtract:**
• Federal income taxes
• Nondeductible penalties/fines
• Life insurance premiums

**Different depreciation methods may apply**
flashcards.formula: E&P = Taxable Income + Tax-exempt - Taxes - Non-deductibles

flashcards.front: When is a stock redemption treated as a sale (not dividend)?
flashcards.back:
**Sale Treatment if:**
• Not essentially equivalent to dividend
• Substantially disproportionate (50% and voting tests)
• Complete termination
• Redemption to pay death taxes (§303)
• Partial liquidation

**Otherwise:** Dividend treatment (if E&P)

**Family attribution rules apply (§318)**

flashcards.front: What are the corporate estimated tax rules?
flashcards.back:
**Quarterly Payments:**
• April 15, June 15, Sept 15, Dec 15 (calendar year)

**Safe Harbor:**
• Pay 100% of current year OR
• Pay 100% of prior year (small corps only)

**Large Corporations:**
• Prior year AGI > $1 million
• Only 1st quarter uses prior year

**Penalty:** Underpayment interest

flashcards.front: What are the penalties for failing to file information returns?
flashcards.back:
**Tiered Penalties (per return):**
• ≤30 days late: $60
• 31 days - Aug 1: $120
• After Aug 1/not filed: $310

**Small Business Max:**
• $220,500 / $630,500 / $1,261,000

**Intentional Disregard:** $630+ or higher

**Applies to:** W-2, 1099, K-1, etc.

flashcards.front: What is the Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction?
flashcards.back:
**Deduction:** Lesser of:
• 20% of QBI, OR
• 20% of taxable income (before QBI)

**Limitations (above threshold):**
• W-2 wages/capital limit
• SSTB phase-out

**2024 Thresholds:**
• Single: $182,100 - $232,100
• MFJ: $364,200 - $464,200

**SSTB:** No deduction above threshold
flashcards.formula: QBI Deduction = Lesser of 20% × QBI or 20% × (TI - Net CG)

flashcards.front: What is a Specified Service Trade or Business (SSTB)?
flashcards.back:
**SSTB Categories:**
• Health, law, accounting
• Actuarial science, performing arts
• Consulting, athletics
• Financial services, brokerage
• Any trade where principal asset is reputation/skill

**NOT SSTB:**
• Engineering, architecture

**Effect:** No QBI deduction above income threshold

flashcards.front: How do SEP-IRA and SIMPLE IRA compare?
flashcards.back:
**SEP-IRA:**
• Employer contributions only
• Up to 25% of compensation
• Max: $69,000 (2024)
• Flexible contributions

**SIMPLE IRA:**
• Employee + employer
• Employee: $16,000 (+$3,500 catch-up)
• Employer: 3% match or 2% nonelective
• ≤100 employees
• 25% penalty first 2 years

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 401(k) contribution limits?
flashcards.back:
**Employee Deferral:** $23,000

**Catch-up (50+):** $7,500 additional

**Total Annual Limit:** $69,000 (employer + employee)

**Compensation Limit:** $345,000

**Highly Compensated:** >$155,000

**ADP/ACP Testing:** Nondiscrimination requirements

flashcards.front: What are the required tax year rules for pass-through entities?
flashcards.back: **Partnerships:**\n1. Majority interest partners\

flashcards.front: When does a short tax year occur?
flashcards.back:
**Causes:**
• Business begins mid-year
• Business terminates mid-year
• Change in accounting period
• Entity election (S corp)

**Annualizing Required:**
• For estimated tax purposes
• For certain threshold tests

**Due Date:** 15th day of 4th month after year-end (partnerships: 15th of 3rd month)

flashcards.front: What is a controlled group of corporations?
flashcards.back:
**Parent-Subsidiary:**
• 80%+ ownership chain

**Brother-Sister:**
• 5 or fewer persons
• >50% common ownership (identical ownership test)
• 80% ownership (combined group)

**Combined Group:**
• Parent-subsidiary + brother-sister

**Effect:**
• Single $250K accumulated earnings credit
• Single graduated rate benefits (pre-TCJA)

flashcards.front: What are the Section 267 related party transaction rules?
flashcards.back:
**Loss Disallowance:**
No deduction for loss on sale to related party

**Related Parties Include:**
• Family members
• >50% owned entity
• Corporation and 50%+ shareholder

**Matching Rule:**
Deduction when included in related party\

flashcards.front: What is Subpart F income?
flashcards.back:
**Controlled Foreign Corporation:**
• >50% owned by US shareholders (10%+)

**Subpart F Income:**
• Foreign base company income
• Insurance income
• Certain passive income

**Effect:**
U.S. shareholders taxed currently (no deferral)

**GILTI:** Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income (separate regime)

flashcards.front: How do you calculate a partner\
flashcards.back:
**Starting Basis:**
Initial contribution (cash + FMV property) + Share of liabilities

**Annual Adjustments:**
**INCREASES:**
+ Distributive share of income
+ Tax-exempt income
+ Additional contributions
+ Increased share of liabilities

**DECREASES:**
- Distributions received
- Distributive share of losses
- Nondeductible expenses
- Decreased share of liabilities

**Basis cannot go below zero**
flashcards.formula: Ending Basis = Beginning + Income + Contributions + Liability↑ - Distributions - Losses - Liability↓

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 Section 179 expensing limits?
flashcards.back:
**Maximum Deduction:** $1,220,000

**Phase-out Threshold:** Begins at $3,050,000
- Reduces dollar-for-dollar above threshold
- Completely eliminated at $4,270,000

**Limitations:**
- Cannot exceed business income
- Per asset limits for vehicles
- Excess carries forward

**Qualifying Property:**
- Tangible personal property
- Qualified improvement property
- Certain real property (roofs, HVAC, fire protection, security)

**SUV limit: $30,500**

flashcards.front: How to remember the order of corporate distribution taxation?
flashcards.back:
**D-R-C = Dividend, Return, Capital Gain**

**Distribution Treatment Order:**
1. **D**ividend - To extent of E&P (current then accumulated)
2. **R**eturn of capital - Tax-free reduction of stock basis
3. **C**apital gain - After basis reduced to zero

**E&P Layers:**
- Current E&P allocated pro-rata to all distributions
- Then accumulated E&P (chronologically)

**Remember: "DRC" - like Democratic Republic... of Cash!**

flashcards.front: What is the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty and who is liable?
flashcards.back:
**Trust Fund Taxes:**
- Withheld income tax
- Employee portion of FICA
- NOT employer FICA portion

**Penalty = 100%** of unpaid trust fund taxes

**Responsible Persons:**
- Officers, directors
- Employees with financial control
- Anyone who can decide which creditors to pay

**Requirements:**
1. **Responsibility** - Authority over finances
2. **Willfulness** - Knew taxes unpaid, chose to pay others

**Multiple persons can be liable for same tax**

flashcards.front: What is the difference between AAA and shareholder stock basis?
flashcards.back:
**AAA (Accumulated Adjustments Account):**
- Corporate-level account
- Tracks S corp earnings since election
- Used to determine dividend treatment
- CAN go negative (but not for tax-free distrib.)
- Reduced by distributions

**Stock Basis:**
- Shareholder-level
- Limits loss deductions
- CANNOT go below zero
- Determines gain on sale
- Includes debt basis (if applicable)

**Key:** Distributions tax-free to extent of AAA but limited by stock basis

flashcards.front: When must a business use the accrual method of accounting?
flashcards.back:
**Accrual Required for:**
- C corporations* with avg gross receipts >$30M
- Tax shelters
- Certain farming operations

***Exception:** Small business ($30M test) can use cash

**Cash Method Allowed if:**
- Average gross receipts ≤$30M (3-yr avg)
- Not a tax shelter
- Sole proprietors, S corps, partnerships generally qualify

**Inventory Exception:**
- Small businesses can treat inventory as non-incidental supplies if <$30M test met

**Gross receipts test is per-entity**

flashcards.front: What is a Sole Proprietorship?
flashcards.back:
**Single-owner unincorporated business**

• Reported on Schedule C (or C-EZ)/Schedule F
• Business income/loss flows to Form 1040
• Subject to Self-Employment Tax
• No separate entity tax return
• Owner has unlimited personal liability
• Simple formation - no state filing required

flashcards.front: What is a Partnership for tax purposes?
flashcards.back:
**Two or more persons carrying on a business for profit**

• Files Form 1065 (informational return)
• Pass-through entity - no entity-level tax
• K-1s issued to partners
• Partners pay tax on distributive share
• General partners pay SE tax
• Limited partners: No SE tax on distributive share

flashcards.front: What are the key characteristics of a C Corporation?
flashcards.back:
**Separate legal entity with double taxation**

• Files Form 1120
• Corporate tax rate: 21% (flat)
• Dividends taxed again to shareholders
• Unlimited shareholders allowed
• Multiple classes of stock allowed
• Full range of fringe benefits deductible
• Fiscal year allowed

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for S Corporation status?
flashcards.back:
**S Corporation Requirements:**

• Domestic corporation
• ≤ 100 shareholders
• Only individuals, estates, certain trusts
• No nonresident alien shareholders
• One class of stock only
• Not an ineligible corporation (bank, insurance)

**Files Form 1120-S** (informational)
**Pass-through taxation** via K-1s

flashcards.front: How is an LLC taxed by default?
flashcards.back:
**Default Classification (Check-the-Box):**

• Single-member LLC: Disregarded entity (Schedule C)
• Multi-member LLC: Partnership (Form 1065)

**Elections available:**
• Elect to be taxed as C corporation
• Elect to be taxed as S corporation

**State law:** Provides liability protection
**Tax law:** IRS ignores the LLC and follows election

flashcards.front: What is MACRS and what are the recovery periods?
flashcards.back:
**Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System**

**Recovery Periods:**
• 3-year: Tractors, racehorses
• 5-year: Autos, computers, equipment
• 7-year: Office furniture, fixtures
• 15-year: Land improvements, QIP
• 27.5-year: Residential rental property
• 39-year: Nonresidential real property

**Methods:** 200% DB (3,5,7,10), 150% DB (15,20), S/L (real property)

flashcards.front: What is the Section 179 expense election?
flashcards.back:
**Immediate expensing of qualifying property**

**2024 Limits:**
• Maximum deduction: $1,220,000
• Investment limit: $3,050,000
• Dollar-for-dollar phaseout above limit

**Qualifying property:** Tangible personal property, off-the-shelf software, qualified real property improvements

**Cannot create or increase a loss** - limited to taxable income from active business

flashcards.front: What is Bonus Depreciation and what are the current rates?
flashcards.back:
**Additional first-year depreciation**

**TCJA Rates (phasing down):**
• 2022: 100%
• 2023: 80%
• 2024: 60%
• 2025: 40%
• 2026: 20%
• 2027+: 0%

**Applies to:** New and used property with recovery period ≤ 20 years
**Can create a loss** (unlike §179)
**Elect out** on property-by-property class basis

flashcards.front: What is Listed Property and what special rules apply?
flashcards.back:
**Property prone to personal use:**
• Passenger autos
• Computers (if not used exclusively at regular business location)
• Cell phones (removed from list in 2010)

**Special rules:**
• Must meet >50% business use for accelerated depreciation
• If <50%: Must use straight-line, ADS
• Luxury auto limits apply
• Substantiation required for business use

flashcards.front: What makes an expense deductible as a business expense?
flashcards.back:
**Must be ORDINARY and NECESSARY:**

**Ordinary:** Common and accepted in your trade/business

**Necessary:** Appropriate and helpful for your business

**Additional requirements:**
• Directly connected to business
• Reasonable in amount
• Not lavish or extravagant
• Properly documented

flashcards.front: What is the current deduction for business meals?
flashcards.back:
**50% deductible** for business meals (general rule)

**Requirements:**
• Taxpayer or employee present
• Food/beverage not lavish
• Business purpose (before, during, or after substantial business discussion)

**100% deductible:**
• Employer-provided meals for convenience (de minimis)
• Recreational events for employees

**0% deductible:**
• Entertainment expenses (post-2017)

flashcards.front: How are business start-up costs treated?
flashcards.back:
**Election to deduct:**
• Up to $5,000 in first year
• Reduced by amount exceeding $50,000

**Remainder:** Amortize over 180 months

**Start-up costs include:**
• Investigation costs
• Business creation costs
• Pre-opening advertising
• Training employees
