**Organizational costs:** Same rules apply

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Cash and Accrual accounting methods?
flashcards.back:
**Cash Method:**
• Income: When received (or constructively received)
• Expenses: When paid
• Simpler, most individuals and small businesses

**Accrual Method:**
• Income: When earned (all events test + economic performance)
• Expenses: When liability fixed and determinable
• Required for: C corps >$30M, inventory (generally), tax shelters

flashcards.front: What is a partner\
flashcards.back: **Partner\

flashcards.front: How is a partner\
flashcards.back:
**Initial Basis:** Cash + adjusted basis of property contributed

**Increased by:**
• Share of income
• Share of tax-exempt income
• Additional contributions
• Increased share of liabilities

**Decreased by:**
• Share of losses (not below zero)
• Non-deductible expenses
• Distributions
• Decreased share of liabilities

flashcards.front: How is an S corporation shareholder\
flashcards.back:
**Initial Basis:** Cost of stock

**Increased by:**
• Pro rata share of income
• Pro rata share of tax-exempt income
• Additional contributions

**Decreased by (in order):**
1. Distributions (not below zero)
2. Non-deductible expenses
3. Deductible losses

**Debt basis:** Separate calculation for loans TO corporation

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 FICA tax rates and wage bases?
flashcards.back:
**Social Security:**
• Rate: 6.2% employee + 6.2% employer = 12.4%
• Wage base: $168,600 (2024)

**Medicare:**
• Rate: 1.45% employee + 1.45% employer = 2.9%
• No wage limit

**Additional Medicare Tax:**
• 0.9% employee-only on wages > $200,000

**Self-Employment:** 15.3% total (with 92.35% adjustment)

flashcards.front: What is the FUTA tax?
flashcards.back:
**Federal Unemployment Tax Act**

**Rate:** 6.0% on first $7,000 of wages per employee

**Credit:** Up to 5.4% for state unemployment taxes paid

**Effective rate:** 0.6% (with full credit)

**Due dates:**
• Deposit quarterly if >$500 liability
• Form 940 due January 31

**Employer-only tax** (not withheld from employee)

flashcards.front: What is the Accumulated Earnings Tax?
flashcards.back:
**Penalty tax on C corps that accumulate earnings to avoid dividends**

**Rate:** 20% on accumulated taxable income

**Credit:** $250,000 ($150,000 for service corps)
• Earnings above this need reasonable business needs

**Reasonable needs:**
• Business expansion
• Working capital
• Debt retirement
• Acquisitions

**Not applicable to:** S corps, tax-exempt entities, PHCs

flashcards.front: What is the Personal Holding Company Tax?
flashcards.back:
**Penalty tax on closely-held investment companies**

**Rate:** 20% on undistributed PHC income

**PHC Definition:**
1. >60% of AGI is PHC income (dividends, rents, royalties, etc.)
2. >50% of stock owned by 5 or fewer individuals

**Avoidance:** Pay dividends (including consent dividends)

**Purpose:** Prevent using corporations to defer passive income

flashcards.front: Partnership Basis Calculation
flashcards.back: Initial Basis + Contributions + Income Share + Increase in Liab Share - Distributions - Loss Share - Decrease in Liab Share

flashcards.front: Outside Basis vs Inside Basis
flashcards.back: Outside: Partner\

flashcards.front: Form 1065 Due Date
flashcards.back: March 15th (for calendar year partnerships). Extension: 6 months (Sept 15).

flashcards.front: Guaranteed Payments
flashcards.back: Payments to partners for services or capital use, made regardless of partnership income. Ordinary income to partner, deductible by partnership.

flashcards.front: S-Corp Eligibility: Shareholder Limit
flashcards.back: Max 100 shareholders (Family members can count as 1).

flashcards.front: S-Corp Eligibility: Shareholder Type
flashcards.back: US Citizens, Residents, certain Trusts/Estates. NO Partnerships, C-Corps, or Non-resident Aliens.

flashcards.front: S-Corp Eligibility: Stock Class
flashcards.back: Only one class of stock allowed (differences in voting rights permitted).

flashcards.front: Form 2553 Deadline
flashcards.back: Must file by March 15th (15th day of 3rd month) to be effective for current year.

flashcards.front: S-Corp Shareholder Basis Order
flashcards.back:
1. Increase for income
2. Decrease for distributions
3. Decrease for non-deductible expenses
4. Decrease for deductible losses/expenses

flashcards.front: Reasonable Compensation
flashcards.back: S-Corps must pay shareholder-employees a "reasonable salary" (subject to FICA) before non-wage distributions.

flashcards.front: Form 1120 Due Date
flashcards.back: April 15th (for calendar year corps). June 30 FYE exception: due Sept 15.

flashcards.front: Capital Contributions
flashcards.back: Generally tax-free to corporation (Section 118) and shareholder (Section 351) if control requirement (80%) met immediately after.

flashcards.front: Net Operating Loss (NOL) - Post-2017
flashcards.back: NOLs can only offset 80% of taxable income. Carryforward indefinite, no carryback (except farming).

flashcards.front: MACRS Life - 3 Years
flashcards.back: Tractor units, racehorses > 2yo, specialized handling devices

flashcards.front: MACRS Life - 5 Years
flashcards.back: Autos, taxis, computers, office machinery, appliances (residential rental)

flashcards.front: MACRS Life - 7 Years
flashcards.back: Office furniture/fixtures, agricultural machinery, equipment not otherwise classified

flashcards.front: MACRS Life - 27.5 Years
flashcards.back: Residential Rental Property

flashcards.front: MACRS Life - 39 Years
flashcards.back: Non-residential Real Property

flashcards.front: Bonus Depreciation (2024)
flashcards.back: 60% deduction allowed for qualified property (down from 80% in 2023).

flashcards.front: Section 179 Limit (2024)
flashcards.back: Max deduction approx $1.22M, phaseout starts at $3.05M investment.

flashcards.front: Section 179 Restrictions
flashcards.back: Limited to taxable income from trade/business (cannot create loss); tangible personal property only (generally).

flashcards.front: Mid-Quarter Convention
flashcards.back: Required if >40% of tangible personal property placed in service during Q4.

flashcards.front: Business Meals Deduction
flashcards.back: 50% deductible (was 100% temporary for 2021-2022 restaurants). Entertainment is 0% deductible.

flashcards.front: Start-up Costs Deduction
flashcards.back: Deduct up to $5,000 immediately (reduced if total >$50k), amortize remainder over 180 months.

flashcards.front: UNICAP (Section 263A)
flashcards.back: Small business exception: Avg annual gross receipts <= $30M (inflation adj) last 3 years exempt from UNICAP.

flashcards.front: Form 941 Due Dates
flashcards.back: Last day of month following quarter end (Apr 30, Jul 31, Oct 31, Jan 31).

flashcards.front: FUTA Rate and Wage Base
flashcards.back: 6.0% on first $7,000 wages. (Max credit of 5.4% usually results in 0.6% effective rate).

flashcards.front: SEP IRA Deadline
flashcards.back: Due date of return + extensions.

flashcards.front: SIMPLE IRA Employee Contribution
flashcards.back: Employees can contribute up to $16,000 (2024).

flashcards.front: Form 1041 Filing Requirement
flashcards.back: Required if gross income >= $600 or any non-resident alien beneficiary.

flashcards.front: Simple Trust Characteristics
flashcards.back:
1. Distributes all income currently
2. Makes no charitable contributions
3. Does not distribute corpus

flashcards.front: Estate Tax Filing Threshold (2024)
flashcards.back: $13.61 Million gross estate

flashcards.front: Annual Gift Exclusion (2024)
flashcards.back: $18,000 per donee

flashcards.front: How do you calculate Double Declining Balance depreciation?
flashcards.back:
**DDB Rate = (1/Life) × 2**

**Annual Depreciation = Beginning Book Value × DDB Rate**

• Never depreciate below salvage value
• Switch to S/L when S/L > DDB for remaining life

**MACRS uses 200% DB** for 3, 5, 7, 10-year property with half-year convention built into tables

flashcards.front: What are the key MACRS depreciation percentages?
flashcards.back:
**5-Year Property (Half-Year):**
Yr 1: 20% | Yr 2: 32% | Yr 3: 19.2% | Yr 4: 11.52% | Yr 5: 11.52% | Yr 6: 5.76%

**7-Year Property (Half-Year):**
Yr 1: 14.29% | Yr 2: 24.49% | Yr 3: 17.49% | Yr 4: 12.49%...

**39-Year (Mid-Month):**
Monthly rate: 2.564%/month × months in service

flashcards.front: What are the 2024 luxury automobile depreciation limits?
flashcards.back:
**Without Bonus Depreciation:**
• Year 1: $12,400
• Year 2: $19,800
• Year 3: $11,900
• Year 4+: $7,160

**With 60% Bonus (2024):**
• Year 1: $20,400
• Years 2-4+: Same as above

**SUV Exception:** Vehicles >6,000 lbs GVWR not subject to limits
flashcards.formula: Lower of: MACRS depreciation OR Luxury limit

flashcards.front: What is the formula for calculating partner"s outside basis?
flashcards.back:
**Beginning Basis**
+ Share of income (ordinary and separately stated)
+ Share of tax-exempt income
+ Partner contributions
+ Increase in share of liabilities
− Distributions received
− Share of losses and deductions
− Share of nondeductible expenses
− Decrease in share of liabilities
= **Ending Basis** (cannot go below zero)
flashcards.formula: Ending Basis = Beginning + (Income − Losses − Distributions) ± Liability Change

flashcards.front: How are partnership liabilities allocated to partners?
flashcards.back:
**Recourse Liabilities:**
Allocated to partners who bear economic risk of loss

**Nonrecourse Liabilities (3-tier):**
1. First: To partners with minimum gain
2. Second: Based on §704(c) minimum gain
3. Third: By profit-sharing ratios

**General rule for simple partnerships:**
Recourse → General partners
Nonrecourse → All partners by profit share

flashcards.front: What are "hot assets" and how do they affect partnership sales?
flashcards.back:
**Hot Assets (IRC §751):**
• Unrealized receivables
• Inventory (substantially appreciated)

**Effect on Sale of Partnership Interest:**
Gain/loss attributable to hot assets = Ordinary income/loss

**Substantially Appreciated Inventory:**
FMV > 120% of adjusted basis
flashcards.formula: Ordinary gain = Share of hot asset appreciation

flashcards.front: What is the formula for S corporation shareholder basis?
flashcards.back:
**Stock Basis Calculation (Order matters!):**

Beginning Stock Basis
+ Income items (ordinary and separately stated)
+ Tax-exempt income
+ Excess depletion
− Distributions (limited to basis)
− Non-deductible expenses
− Deductible losses (limited to remaining basis)
= Ending Stock Basis

**Debt Basis:** Only from direct shareholder loans to corp
flashcards.formula: Stock Basis + Debt Basis = Total Loss Deduction Limit

flashcards.front: What is the Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA)?
flashcards.back:
**AAA tracks S corp earnings that can be distributed tax-free**

**Calculation:**
Prior AAA Balance
+ Ordinary income/Separately stated income
− Ordinary loss/Deductions
− Distributions (limited to AAA)
= Ending AAA

**Note:** Tax-exempt income increases stock basis but NOT AAA
**AAA can go negative** (from losses, not distributions)
flashcards.formula: AAA = Prior AAA + Income − Losses − Distributions

flashcards.front: What is the charitable contribution limit for C corporations?
flashcards.back:
**Limit: 10% of Taxable Income**

**Taxable Income computed WITHOUT:**
• Charitable contribution deduction
• DRD (Dividends Received Deduction)
• NOL carryback
• Capital loss carryback

**Carryover:** Excess carries forward 5 years

flashcards.front: What are the Dividends Received Deduction (DRD) percentages?
flashcards.back:
**DRD Based on Ownership %:**

• <20% ownership: 50% DRD
• 20%-79% ownership: 65% DRD
• 80%+ ownership: 100% DRD (affiliated group)

**Limitation:** Cannot exceed same % of taxable income (unless creates/increases NOL)

**Purpose:** Prevent triple taxation of corporate earnings

flashcards.front: How is corporate Earnings and Profits (E&P) calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Taxable Income**
+ Tax-exempt income
+ Life insurance proceeds
+ Excess percentage depletion
+ DRD adjustment (add back)
− Federal income taxes
− Nondeductible penalties/fines
− Related party losses denied
− Excess capital losses
± Depreciation adjustment (E&P uses S/L, ADS)
= **Current E&P**

flashcards.front: How is Cost of Goods Sold calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Beginning Inventory**
+ Purchases
+ Labor
+ Materials/Supplies
+ Other Costs
= **Cost of Goods Available**
− Ending Inventory
= **Cost of Goods Sold**
flashcards.formula: COGS = Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Ending Inventory

flashcards.front: What is the simplified method for home office deduction?
flashcards.back:
**Simplified Method:**
$5 per square foot (max 300 sq ft)
= **Maximum $1,500 deduction**

**Regular Method:**
(Business sq ft ÷ Total sq ft) × Actual Expenses

**Actual expenses include:**
Mortgage interest, taxes, insurance, utilities, repairs, depreciation

flashcards.front: What are the two methods for deducting vehicle expenses?
flashcards.back:
**Standard Mileage Rate (2024):**
• Business: 67¢ per mile
• Medical/Moving: 21¢ per mile
• Charity: 14¢ per mile

**Actual Expense Method:**
(Business miles ÷ Total miles) × Actual costs

**Actual costs:** Gas, insurance, repairs, depreciation, registration

**Must use actual method if:** Fleet of 5+ or claimed depreciation in prior years

flashcards.front: What is the maximum SEP-IRA contribution for self-employed?
flashcards.back:
**Employees:** 25% of compensation (up to $69,000 in 2024)

**Self-Employed (more complex):**

**Step 1:** Net SE income × 92.35%
**Step 2:** Result × 20% (not 25%!)

**Why 20%?** The deduction itself reduces SE income, so effective rate is 20%

**Max contribution:** $69,000 (2024)

flashcards.front: How to remember the key characteristics for entity selection?
flashcards.back:
**"FLIPS"** - Factors for entity selection:

• **F**ormation costs and complexity
• **L**iability protection
• **I**ncome tax treatment (pass-through vs double tax)
• **P**rofit/loss allocation flexibility
• **S**elf-employment tax exposure

flashcards.front: What are the S corporation eligibility requirements?
flashcards.back:
**"DRIPS"** - S Corp Requirements:

• **D**omestic corporation only
• **R**esidents/Citizens as shareholders (no NRAs)
• **I**ndividuals, estates, certain trusts only
• **P**rohibited: No other C corps or partnerships as shareholders
• **S**tock class: Only ONE class of stock

**Also:** Max 100 shareholders, no prohibited corps (banks, insurance)

flashcards.front: What can be contributed to a partnership tax-free?
flashcards.back:
**"MAPS"** - Tax-free partnership contributions:

• **M**oney (cash)
• **A**ssets (property at carryover basis)
• **P**roperty encumbered (watch for liability shifts!)
• **S**ervices? **NO!** Services are taxable

**Key rule:** Services taxable at FMV, property tax-free (unless liabilities exceed basis)

flashcards.front: How to remember common MACRS recovery periods?
flashcards.back:
**"3-5-7 COW"**

• **3** years: Certain manufacturing tools, horses
• **5** years: **C**omputers, **C**ars, **C**opiers, office equipment
• **7** years: **O**ffice furniture, fixtures
• **W**arehouse/buildings: 27.5 (residential) or 39 (commercial)

**Memory tip:** The smaller/moveable, the shorter the life

flashcards.front: How to remember Section 179 limitations?
flashcards.back:
**"BITS"** - Section 179 Limitations:

• **B**usiness income limit (cannot create loss)
• **I**nvestment ceiling before phase-out ($3,050,000 in 2024)
• **T**otal expense limit ($1,220,000 in 2024)
• **S**ervice placed in tax year required

**Dollar-for-dollar phase-out** above investment threshold

flashcards.front: What qualifies as listed property with stricter rules?
flashcards.back:
**"PEACE"** - Listed Property:

• **P**assenger automobiles
• **E**ntertainment equipment
• **A**ircraft (non-commercial)
• **C**omputers (unless exclusive business use)
• **E**quipment for entertainment/recreation

**Rule:** Must be >50% business use for MACRS; otherwise use ADS straight-line

flashcards.front: What makes an expense deductible under §162?
flashcards.back:
**"CORN"** - Business Expense Test:

• **C**ommon and accepted in the industry
• **O**rdinary (not unusual)
• **R**easonable in amount
• **N**ecessary (helpful and appropriate)

**All four must be met** for deductibility

flashcards.front: What common business expenses are NOT deductible?
flashcards.back:
**"PIBB"** - Nondeductible Expenses:

• **P**enalties and fines (government-imposed)
• **I**llegal bribes and kickbacks
• **B**ribes to government officials
• **B**usiness meals: Only 50% (except 2021-22 was 100%)

**Also nondeductible:** Political contributions, club dues, commuting

flashcards.front: What are the startup cost deduction rules?
flashcards.back:
**"5-5-50-180"** Rule:

• **$5,000** immediate deduction allowed
• **$5,000** for organizational costs (separate limit)
• **$50,000** - Phase-out threshold (dollar-for-dollar reduction)
• **180** months - Amortization period for excess

**Key:** Each $5K limit reduced by amount over $50K spent

flashcards.front: What is the order of S corporation basis adjustments?
flashcards.back:
**"I-D-E-L"** - S Corp Basis Order:

1. **I**ncome (increases basis first)
2. **D**istributions (reduce after income)
3. **E**xpenses (nondeductible, reduce basis)
4. **L**osses (last; limited to remaining basis)

**Why order matters:** Losses deducted last, so income increases cushion for loss deduction

flashcards.front: What are the four loss limitation hurdles for S corp shareholders?
flashcards.back:
**"BARE"** - S Corp Loss Hurdles (in order):

1. **B**asis - Stock + direct debt only
2. **A**t-risk - What you could lose
3. **R**isk/Passive - PAL rules (material participation)
4. **E**xcess business loss - TCJA limitation

**Must pass each hurdle** before loss is deductible

flashcards.front: What makes up FICA taxes?
flashcards.back:
**"S-M"** - FICA Components:

• **S**ocial Security: 6.2% (employer + employee)
  - Wage base: $168,600 (2024)
  
• **M**edicare: 1.45% (employer + employee)
  - NO wage limit
  - Additional 0.9% on wages >$200K (employee only)

**Total employer cost:** 7.65% of wages

flashcards.front: What are the key FUTA facts to remember?
flashcards.back:
**"6-5.4-7"** FUTA Formula:

• **6%** gross rate
• **5.4%** credit (if timely state payments)
• **0.6%** net federal rate
• **$7,000** wage base per employee

**Max FUTA per employee:** $7,000 × 0.006 = $42

**Quarterly deposits** required if liability >$500

flashcards.front: When must a taxpayer use the accrual method?
flashcards.back:
**"PICS"** - Must use Accrual:

• **P**urchase/sale of inventory (with exceptions)
• **I**ncome >$31 million (gross receipts test)
• **C** corporations (generally)
• **S**helters (tax shelters always accrual)

**Small taxpayer exception:** Average gross receipts ≤$31M can use cash

flashcards.front: What form and process is needed to change accounting methods?
flashcards.back:
**"3115 to the IRS"**

• **Form 3115** - Application to Change Accounting Method
• File with **return** for year of change (plus copy to IRS)
• Most changes require **§481(a) adjustment**

**Positive adjustment:** Taken over 4 years
**Negative adjustment:** Taken in full in year of change

flashcards.front: Who may practice before the IRS under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Unlimited Practice:**
• Attorneys
• CPAs
• Enrolled Agents
• Enrolled Actuaries (employee plans)
• Enrolled Retirement Plan Agents

**Limited Practice:**
• Annual Filing Season Program participants (limited representation)
• Unenrolled preparers (limited, own clients)

**Must be in good standing and not under suspension/disbarment**

flashcards.front: What are the requirements to become and remain an Enrolled Agent?
flashcards.back:
**Become EA:**
• Pass all 3 parts of SEE exam, OR
• Former IRS employee (5+ years technical experience)
• Pass background check

**Maintain Status:**
• Complete 72 hours CE every 3 years
• Minimum 16 hours per year
• 2 hours ethics per year
• Timely renewal (annually, Suitability checks)

flashcards.front: What are the due diligence requirements under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Due Diligence Duties:**
• Determine correctness of oral/written representations to Treasury
• Determine correctness of oral/written representations to clients
• Exercise due diligence in preparing and filing documents
• Cannot unreasonably delay prompt disposition of matters

**Reliance on Clients:**
May rely in good faith on client information if reasonable questions asked

flashcards.front: What are the conflict of interest rules under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Cannot represent:**
Clients with conflicting interests unless:

**Written Consent Required:**
• Competent representation possible
• Not prohibited by law
• Each affected client gives informed consent
• Confirmed in writing within 30 days

**Retained for 36 months after conclusion**

flashcards.front: What are the fee rules under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Reasonable Fees:**
May charge reasonable fee for professional services

**Prohibited:**
• Unconscionable fees
• Contingent fees for:
  - Original returns
  - Amended returns (unless with refund claim)
  - Claims for credit or refund

**Contingent Fees Allowed:**
• IRS examination/challenge matters
• Judicial proceedings
• Whistleblower claims

flashcards.front: What are the advertising and solicitation rules?
flashcards.back:
**Permitted Advertising:**
• Must not be false, fraudulent, misleading, or deceptive
• May publish fee schedules
• May use professional designations (EA, CPA, etc.)

**Prohibited:**
• Using "certified" unless officially certified
• Implying special relationships with IRS
• Uninvited solicitation in federal buildings
• Coercive conduct toward clients

flashcards.front: What are the rules for returning client records?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:**
Must promptly return client records upon request

**Records Include:**
• Documents provided by client
• Documents necessary for compliance

**May Retain:**
• If permitted by state law
• For unpaid fees (if state permits)
• Work product (not client records)

**Disputes:** Cannot withhold records needed for compliance

flashcards.front: What must a practitioner do upon discovering an error on a client\
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Promptly advise client of error or omission
• Advise of consequences under Code

**No Duty to:**
• Report error to IRS
• Amend the return (but must advise client)

**If Client Refuses to Correct:**
• Consider whether to continue representation
• Cannot sign subsequent returns with known errors

flashcards.front: What sanctions can be imposed under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Sanctions (Progressive):**
1. Censure (public or private reprimand)
2. Suspension (cannot practice for specified period)
3. Disbarment (permanent prohibition)
4. Monetary penalty (up to gross income from conduct)

**Expedited Suspension:**
• Criminal conviction
• License loss in state
• Failure to file federal tax returns
• Misappropriation of client funds

flashcards.front: What constitutes disreputable conduct under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Disreputable Conduct Includes:**
• Criminal conviction (tax or dishonesty)
• Giving false information to Treasury
• Soliciting using false or misleading information
• Contempt of court order to produce evidence
• Willfully failing to file returns or pay taxes
• Misappropriating client funds
• Aiding or abetting violations
• Knowingly providing false opinions

flashcards.front: How do Office of Professional Responsibility (OPR) disciplinary proceedings work?
flashcards.back:
**Process:**
1. Investigation by OPR
2. Complaint filed if probable cause
3. Answer within 30 days
4. Hearing before ALJ
5. Decision by ALJ
6. Appeal to Treasury

**Burden:** Government must prove charges

**Standard:** Preponderance of evidence

**Can be reinstated after disbarment (5+ years)**

flashcards.front: What are the Section 6694 preparer penalties?
flashcards.back:
**§6694(a) Unreasonable Positions:**
• Greater of $1,000 or 50% of income from return
• Understated positions without substantial authority

**§6694(b) Willful/Reckless Conduct:**
• Greater of $5,000 or 75% of income from return
• Willful understatement
• Reckless/intentional disregard of rules

**Defense:** Reasonable cause and good faith

flashcards.front: What other penalties apply to tax return preparers?
flashcards.back:
**§6695 Penalties:**
• (a) Failure to furnish copy: $55 per return
• (b) Failure to sign: $55 per return
• (c) Failure to furnish PTIN: $55 per return
• (d) Failure to retain copy: $55 per return
• (e) Failure to file correct info return: $55 per return
• (f) Negotiating refund check: $560 per check
• (g) EITC due diligence: $600 per failure

**Max for (a)-(e): $29,500 per year**

flashcards.front: What are the EITC due diligence requirements for preparers?
flashcards.back:
**Form 8867 Required for:**
• EITC
• Child Tax Credit/ACTC/ODC
• American Opportunity Credit
• Head of Household filing status

**Requirements:**
• Complete eligibility checklist (Form 8867)
• Compute credit/HOH status
• Retain records (3 years)
• Make reasonable inquiries

**Penalty:** $600 per failure (2024)

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for Form 2848 Power of Attorney?
flashcards.back:
**Required Elements:**
• Taxpayer name, address, SSN/EIN
• Representative name, address, CAF number
• Matters (type, form, years)
• Specific authorizations (sign returns, receive refunds, etc.)
• Taxpayer signature and date

**Authorization Period:**
• Specific years/periods listed
• Generally 6 years (or statute period)

**Revokes prior POAs for same matters**

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Form 2848 and Form 8821?
flashcards.back:
**Form 2848 - Power of Attorney:**
• Authorizes representation before IRS
• Can receive/inspect confidential information
• Can perform acts on taxpayer\

flashcards.front: What is the Centralized Authorization File (CAF)?
flashcards.back:
**CAF Number:**
• Unique 9-digit identifier
• Assigned to representatives
• First Form 2848/8821 filed auto-assigns
• Permanent unless revoked

**CAF Database:**
• Tracks all authorizations
• Electronic access for representatives
• Updated when new forms filed

**CAF Unit:** Processes authorization forms

flashcards.front: What is the IRS collection process sequence?
flashcards.back:
**Collection Sequence:**
1. Assessment of tax
2. Notice and demand (30 days)
3. Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL)
4. Final Notice of Intent to Levy (CDP rights)
5. Levy/seizure (if no response/arrangement)

**10-Year Collection Statute:**
Expires 10 years from assessment date
(Can be extended/suspended)

flashcards.front: What is a Federal Tax Lien?
flashcards.back:
**Automatic Lien:**
• Arises when assessment + demand + failure to pay
• Attaches to ALL property and rights to property

**Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL):**
• Public notice (filed with county/state)
• Protects IRS priority vs. other creditors

**Release:** 30 days after full payment or statute expiration

**Subordination/Withdrawal available**

flashcards.front: What are the rules for IRS levies?
flashcards.back:
**Levy Authority:**
• Seize and sell property to satisfy tax debt
• Bank accounts, wages, receivables
• Personal property

**Required Notice:**
30 days before levy (CDP rights given)

**Exempt from Levy:**
• Clothing/schoolbooks
• Fuel, provisions (25 limit)
• Tools of trade ($6,250)
• Unemployment benefits
• Workers\

flashcards.front: What are the types of IRS installment agreements?
flashcards.back:
**Guaranteed IA:**
• ≤$10,000 owed
• Filed all returns past 5 years
• Pay within 3 years

**Streamlined IA:**
• ≤$50,000 owed
• Pay within 72 months
• Direct debit required if $25K-$50K

**Regular IA:**
• >$50,000 or >72 months
• Financial disclosure required (433-A/F)

**User fee applies; penalties/interest continue**

flashcards.front: What are the grounds for an Offer in Compromise?
flashcards.back:
**Three Grounds:**

**1. Doubt as to Liability:**
• Dispute about amount owed

**2. Doubt as to Collectibility:**
• Cannot pay full amount
• RCP < tax liability

**3. Effective Tax Administration:**
• Can pay, but creates economic hardship
• Exceptional circumstances

**RCP = Quick Sale Value of Assets + Future Income**

flashcards.front: How is the Offer in Compromise amount calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP):**

RCP = Asset Equity + Future Income

**Lump Sum Offer:**
• 20% deposit required
• Future income × 12 months

**Periodic Payment:**
• First payment with offer
• Future income × 24 months

**Forms 433-A (OIC) and 656 required**
**$205 application fee**

flashcards.front: What are Collection Due Process (CDP) rights?
flashcards.back:
**CDP Hearing Rights:**
• Right to hearing before lien/levy
• Must request within 30 days of notice
• Form 12153 - CDP or Equivalent Hearing

**Issues at Hearing:**
• Challenge liability (if no prior opportunity)
• Collection alternatives (IA, OIC, CNC)
• Appropriateness of collection action

**Stay on Collection:** While CDP pending

**Appeal to Tax Court within 30 days**

flashcards.front: What is the difference between CDP and Equivalent Hearing?
flashcards.back:
**CDP Hearing:**
• Request within 30 days
• Suspends collection statute
• Right to Tax Court review
• Suspends levy action

**Equivalent Hearing:**
• Request within 1 year
• Does NOT suspend statute
• NO Tax Court review
• Does NOT suspend levy

**Same issues considered at both**

flashcards.front: What are the types of IRS examinations?
flashcards.back:
**Correspondence Audit:**
• Mail-based
• Simple issues (math errors, missing docs)
• Most common

**Office Audit:**
• Taxpayer visits IRS office
• Complex individual issues
• Limited scope

**Field Audit:**
• Agent visits taxpayer location
• Complex returns (business, high income)
• Comprehensive examination

flashcards.front: What is the statute of limitations for IRS assessment?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:** 3 years from filing/due date (later)

**Extended to 6 Years:**
• >25% omission of gross income
• Basis overstatement (certain)

**No Limit:**
• Fraud
• Failure to file
• False return

**Form 872:** Consent to extend
**Form 872-A:** Open-ended extension

flashcards.front: What are the fundamental taxpayer rights (Taxpayer Bill of Rights)?
flashcards.back:
**10 Rights:**
1. Right to be informed
2. Right to quality service
3. Right to pay no more than correct amount
4. Right to challenge IRS and be heard
5. Right to appeal
6. Right to finality
7. Right to privacy
8. Right to confidentiality
9. Right to retain representation
10. Right to a fair and just tax system

**Publication 1 - Your Rights as a Taxpayer**

flashcards.front: Who has the burden of proof in tax controversies?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:**
Taxpayer has burden of proving deductions, credits

**Burden Shifts to IRS if (§7491):**
• Taxpayer produces credible evidence
• Complied with substantiation requirements
• Cooperated with reasonable IRS requests
• Maintains records

**IRS Always Has Burden:**
• Statistics only (unreported income)
• Fraud penalties

flashcards.front: What is the IRS Appeals process?
flashcards.back:
**Administrative Appeal:**
• Independent IRS office
• File protest after 30-day letter
• No new issues unless fraud

**Small Case Request:**
• <$25,000 per period
• Brief written request

**Formal Protest Required:**
• >$25,000
• Specific content requirements

**Appeal Conferences:**
• Settlement discussions
• Hazards of litigation considered

flashcards.front: What must a formal protest contain?
flashcards.back:
**Required Elements:**
• Statement that it is a protest
• Taxpayer name, address, contact info
• Copy of IRS letter/date
• Tax years/periods involved
• Itemized adjustments (agree/disagree)
• Facts supporting position
• Law/authority supporting position
• Penalties of perjury statement
• Signature

**Submit within 30 days of IRS letter**

flashcards.front: What ADR options are available for tax disputes?
flashcards.back:
**Fast Track Settlement:**
• Exam or Collection cases
• Appeals officer mediates
• 60 days to resolve

**Post-Appeals Mediation:**
• After Appeals conference
• Non-binding
• Joint session with mediator

**Arbitration:**
• Binding
• Limited issues (factual only)
• By agreement of parties

flashcards.front: What are the options for judicial review of tax disputes?
flashcards.back:
**U.S. Tax Court:**
• Pre-payment forum
• Must file within 90 days of notice of deficiency
• Small Tax Case: ≤$50,000 (no appeal)

**District Court:**
• Pay first, then sue for refund
• Jury trial available

**Court of Federal Claims:**
• Pay first, then sue for refund
• No jury trial

**Appeal:** Circuit Court (except CFC → Federal Circuit)

flashcards.front: What is a Notice of Deficiency (90-day letter)?
flashcards.back: **Statutory Notice:**\n• IRS\

flashcards.front: What constitutes reasonable cause for penalty abatement?
flashcards.back:
**Reasonable Cause Factors:**
• Death or serious illness
• Unavoidable absence
• Fire, casualty, natural disaster
• Inability to obtain records
• Advice from IRS employee
• Advice from tax professional (reliance)

**Must Show:**
• Ordinary business care exercised
• Unable to comply despite efforts

**First Time Abatement (FTA):** Clean history 3 years

flashcards.front: What is the First Time Abatement (FTA) policy?
flashcards.back:
**Eligibility:**
• No penalties in prior 3 years
• All required returns filed
• All taxes paid (or in IA)

**Applies to:**
• Failure to file
• Failure to pay
• Failure to deposit

**Request:** Call IRS or write
**No Form required**

**Administrative waiver (not in Code)**

flashcards.front: How is IRS interest calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Underpayment Rates (Quarterly):**
• Individuals: Federal short-term + 3%
• Corporations: Federal short-term + 3%
• Large corporate underpayments: +5%

**Overpayment Rates:**
• Individuals: Federal short-term + 3%
• Corporations: Federal short-term + 2%
• Large overpayments: Federal short-term + 0.5%

**Compounded daily**

flashcards.front: What are the procedures for claiming a tax refund?
flashcards.back:
**File Claim:**
• Form 1040-X (individuals)
• Form 1120-X (corporations)

**Required Content:**
• Specific amount claimed
• Specific grounds for refund
• Facts supporting claim

**Statute of Limitations:**
• Later of 3 years from filing or 2 years from payment
• Amount limited by payment timing

**No IRS response in 6 months = may sue**

flashcards.front: What is the statute of limitations for claiming refunds?
flashcards.back:
**File Claim Within Later of:**
• 3 years from original due date/filing, OR
• 2 years from date tax was paid

**Amount Limitations:**
• If filed within 3 years: Taxes paid within 3 years + extension period
• If filed after 3 years: Only taxes paid within 2 years

**Special Rules:** Extended for bad debts, worthless securities (7 years)
flashcards.formula: Deadline = Later of (3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment)

flashcards.front: What are the Section 7216 consent requirements?
flashcards.back:
**§7216 Prohibits:**
Disclosure/use of tax return information for purposes other than preparation

**Consent Requirements:**
• Written, signed, dated
• Purpose clearly stated
• Specific information described
• Who will receive information
• Expiration date (no later than 1 year)

**Penalty:** $1,000 fine and/or imprisonment

flashcards.front: What does Section 6103 provide regarding tax return confidentiality?
flashcards.back:
**General Rule:**
Returns and return information are confidential

**Authorized Disclosures:**
• To taxpayer (or designee)
• To state tax agencies (as authorized)
• To Congressional committees
• For tax administration
• Court orders
• Statistical purposes

**Penalty for Unauthorized Disclosure:** Fine + imprisonment

flashcards.front: What are the standards for tax return positions?
flashcards.back:
**§6694 Preparer Penalty Standard:**
• Substantial authority (general)
• Reasonable basis + disclosure (disclosed positions)
• More likely than not (tax shelters)

**Circular 230 Standard:**
• Reasonable belief that position will more likely than not be sustained (non-frivolous)
• Reasonable cause if challenged

**Good faith reliance on client info**

flashcards.front: What are the standards for written tax advice under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Requirements:**
• Based on reasonable factual and legal assumptions
• Reasonably consider all relevant facts
• Relate law to facts
• Not rely on unreasonable factual assumptions
• Not rely on unreasonable legal assumptions

**Practitioner must use good judgment**

**"Covered opinions" rules eliminated 2014**

flashcards.front: What is the IRS Identity Protection PIN (IP PIN) program?
flashcards.back:
**IP PIN:**
• 6-digit number assigned to taxpayers
• Used to verify identity on return
• Changes annually
• Prevents fraudulent returns

**Eligibility:**
• Identity theft victims (automatically issued)
• Available to all taxpayers (opt-in)
• File Form 15227 or use online tool

**Must include on return or return rejected**

flashcards.front: What is Form 14039 Identity Theft Affidavit?
flashcards.back:
**Purpose:**
Report suspected tax-related identity theft to IRS

**When to File:**
• SSN used to file fraudulent return
• IRS letter about unknown income
• E-file rejected (return already filed)

**Contents:**
• Taxpayer information
• Description of identity theft
• Supporting documentation

**Include copy of ID and proof of identity**

flashcards.front: What are the three types of innocent spouse relief?
flashcards.back:
**1. Innocent Spouse Relief (§6015(b)):**
• Understatement attributable to other spouse
• Did not know and had no reason to know
• Inequitable to hold liable

**2. Separation of Liability (§6015(c)):**
• Divorced/separated or lived apart 12 months
• Allocate deficiency between spouses

**3. Equitable Relief (§6015(f)):**
• Not eligible for (b) or (c)
• Inequitable to hold liable
• All relevant factors considered

flashcards.front: What is Form 8857 and when is it filed?
flashcards.back:
**Form 8857:** Request for Innocent Spouse Relief

**Deadline:**
• Innocent Spouse: 2 years from first collection activity
• Separation of Liability: 2 years from collection activity
• Equitable Relief: No time limit (but reasonable time required)

**IRS must notify other spouse**

**Appeal to Tax Court within 90 days if denied**

flashcards.front: What is the Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS)?
flashcards.back:
**TAS Functions:**
• Independent organization within IRS
• Helps taxpayers resolve problems
• Protects taxpayer rights
• Identifies systemic issues

**When to Contact:**
• IRS not responding
• Facing significant hardship
• Experiencing delays

**Form 911:** Request for Taxpayer Advocate Assistance

**Low Income Taxpayer Clinics (LITC)** also available

flashcards.front: What is Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status?
flashcards.back:
**CNC Status:**
• IRS suspends active collection
• Cannot pay living expenses and tax
• Financial hardship demonstrated

**Requirements:**
• Complete financial disclosure (Form 433-A/433-F)
• Provide documentation
• File all required returns

**Effects:**
• Collection suspended (not forgiven)
• Statute continues to run
• Lien may remain filed
• Reviewed periodically

flashcards.front: When are tax debts dischargeable in bankruptcy?
flashcards.back:
**Income Taxes Dischargeable if:**
• 3-year rule: Due date > 3 years before filing
• 2-year rule: Filed > 2 years before filing
• 240-day rule: Assessed > 240 days before filing
• Not fraud or willful evasion
• Not substitute for return

**Priority Tax Debts:**
• Taxes less than 3 years old
• Trust fund taxes
• Cannot be discharged

flashcards.front: What is Form 433-A Collection Information Statement?
flashcards.back:
**Form 433-A:** Wage earners and self-employed

**Information Required:**
• Personal information
• Employment information
• Income and expenses
• Assets (bank accounts, vehicles, real estate)
• Account balances

**Used For:**
• Installment agreements
• Offer in Compromise
• Currently Not Collectible

**Form 433-B:** Businesses
**Form 433-F:** Simplified version

flashcards.front: What is Form 656 and related OIC forms?
flashcards.back:
**Form 656:** Offer in Compromise

**OIC Package Includes:**
• Form 656 (offer)
• Form 433-A (OIC) for individuals
• Form 433-B (OIC) for businesses
• $205 application fee
• Initial payment (20% lump sum or first payment)

**Form 656-L:** For doubt as to liability only (no financial disclosure, no fee)

flashcards.front: What is Form 9465 Installment Agreement Request?
flashcards.back:
**Form 9465:** Request for monthly payment plan

**Information Required:**
• Tax owed
• Monthly payment amount
• Payment date preference
• Bank information (if direct debit)

**Automatic Acceptance:**
• ≤$50,000 owed
• Payable within 72 months
• All returns filed

**User Fee:** $31-$225 depending on type

flashcards.front: What are the mandatory e-file requirements for preparers?
flashcards.back:
**Mandatory E-File:**
• Preparers filing 11+ covered returns
• Per calendar year
• Applies to income tax returns

**Covered Returns:**
• 1040 series
• 1120/1120-S
• 1065
• 990 (certain)

**Exceptions:**
• Taxpayer elects paper
• Hardship waiver (Form 8944)
• Rejected e-file not timely corrected

flashcards.front: What are the responsibilities of Electronic Return Originators (EROs)?
flashcards.back:
**ERO Duties:**
• Verify taxpayer identity
• Submit returns timely
• Retain records 3 years
• Provide copy to taxpayer
• Use PTIN

**Prohibited:**
• Negotiating/cashing refund checks
• Using knowledge of PIN/AGI for fraud
• Charging separate e-file fee (in some states)

**Form 8879:** IRS e-file Signature Authorization

flashcards.front: What is an IRS closing agreement?
flashcards.back:
**Closing Agreement (§7121):**
• Final determination of tax liability
• Binding on both IRS and taxpayer
• Cannot be reopened except for fraud/malfeasance

**Form 866:** Agreement as to Final Determination

**Form 906:** Closing Agreement on Final Determination

**Benefits:** Certainty, finality, prevents future adjustment on issues covered

flashcards.front: What is a Private Letter Ruling (PLR)?
flashcards.back:
**PLR:**
• Written IRS statement on specific transaction
• Interprets/applies tax law
• Issued by National Office
• Requested by taxpayer

**Binding only on requesting taxpayer**

**User Fee Required:** $3,000+

**Cannot be cited as precedent by others**

**Rev. Proc. 2024-1 governs process**

flashcards.front: What are the key IRS statutory notices?
flashcards.back:
**CP2000:** Proposed adjustment (underreporter)

**30-Day Letter:**
• Proposed deficiency
• Right to appeal

**90-Day Letter (Notice of Deficiency):**
• Statutory notice
• 90 days to petition Tax Court

**Notice of Federal Tax Lien:** CDP rights

**Final Notice of Intent to Levy:** CDP rights

flashcards.front: How do you request penalty abatement?
flashcards.back:
**Methods:**
• Phone (simple cases)
• Written request
• Form 843 (preferred for formal requests)

**Request Must Include:**
• Specific penalties to abate
• Tax year(s) affected
• Reason for abatement
• Supporting documentation

**Grounds:**
• Reasonable cause
• First Time Abatement
• Statutory exception
• Administrative waiver

flashcards.front: What is the IRS Voluntary Disclosure Practice?
flashcards.back:
**Voluntary Disclosure:**
• Coming forward before criminal investigation begins
• Taxpayer makes truthful disclosure
• Cooperates with IRS

**Requirements:**
• Submit preclearance request
• File all original/amended returns
• Pay in full (or make arrangement)
• Cooperate completely

**Benefits:**
• May avoid criminal prosecution
• Civil penalties still apply

**Not guaranteed** - case-by-case review

flashcards.front: What are the requirements for written tax advice under Circular 230 §10.37?
flashcards.back:
**Written Advice Must:**
- Be based on reasonable factual assumptions
- Reasonably consider all relevant facts
- Not rely on unreasonable assumptions
- Not take into consideration audit odds

**Cannot:**
- Base on assumptions you know are incorrect
- Assume favorable resolution of unsettled issues
- Ignore facts that are potentially unfavorable

**Due Diligence Required:**
- Make reasonable inquiry if information seems incomplete
- Document advice and supporting analysis

flashcards.front: What triggers the accuracy-related penalty under IRC §6662?
flashcards.back:
**Penalty: 20% of underpayment**

**Triggers:**
- Negligence or disregard of rules
- Substantial understatement of income tax
- Substantial valuation misstatement
- Overstatement of pension liabilities
- Substantial estate/gift valuation understatement

**Substantial Understatement:**
- Individuals: >$5,000 OR >10% of tax shown
- Corporations: >$10M OR >10% of tax (>$10,000 min)

**Defenses:**
- Reasonable cause and good faith
- Adequate disclosure
- Substantial authority

flashcards.front: How to remember the key IRS statutes of limitations?
flashcards.back:
**"3-6-10 Never" Rule:**

**3 years** - Standard assessment period
**6 years** - 25%+ gross income omission
**10 years** - Collection period (CSED)
**Never** - Fraud (no limit on assessment)

**Memory Aid:**
"The IRS can ASSESS in **3** years,
Send you to **6** if you hide ¼,
Collect for **10** years,
But fraud lasts FOREVER"

**Key Dates:**
- Assessment: Later of due date or filing date
- Collection: Date of assessment

flashcards.front: What are the filing compliance requirements for an Offer in Compromise?
flashcards.back:
**Before Submitting OIC:**
- All required tax returns filed
- Current on estimated tax payments
- Current on federal tax deposits (employers)

**During OIC Consideration:**
- Stay current on all filing obligations
- Make estimated payments
- Make federal tax deposits

**Cannot submit OIC if:**
- Open bankruptcy proceeding
- Unfiled tax returns
- Not current on estimated taxes/deposits

**Low-income waiver:** Fee and payment waived if ≤250% poverty

flashcards.front: Who has limited practice rights before the IRS?
flashcards.back:
**Individuals with Limited Rights:**

**AFSP Participants:**
- Can represent before revenue agents and customer service
- Only for returns they prepared and signed
- Cannot represent before Appeals, Collection, or Counsel

**Unenrolled Return Preparers:**
- No representation rights after 12/31/2015

**Students (LITC/VITA):**
- Under supervision of qualified representative
- Limited to that program

**Full Practice Rights:**
- EAs, CPAs, Attorneys only
- Can represent in all IRS matters

flashcards.front: What are the paid preparer due diligence requirements for credits?
flashcards.back:
**Due Diligence Required for:**
- Earned Income Credit (EIC)
- Child Tax Credit/Additional CTC/Other Dependent Credit
- American Opportunity Credit (AOTC)
- Head of Household filing status

**Requirements:**
1. Complete Form 8867 eligibility checklist
2. Complete applicable worksheets
3. Make reasonable inquiries
4. Retain records for 3 years

**Penalty for Each Failure:** $635 (2024)

**Each credit/status is separate penalty**

**Not imposed if due diligence performed and documented**

flashcards.front: What is Treasury Circular No. 230?
flashcards.back:
**Regulations Governing Practice before the IRS**

**Key provisions:**
• Who may practice before the IRS
• Duties and restrictions for practitioners
• Sanctions for violations
• Disciplinary proceedings

**Subtitle:** "Regulations Governing Practice before the Internal Revenue Service"

flashcards.front: What constitutes "practice before the IRS"?
flashcards.back:
**All matters connected with presentation to the IRS:**

• **Preparing** documents or returns
• **Filing** documents with IRS
• **Corresponding and communicating** with IRS
• **Representing** a client at conferences, hearings
• **Rendering written advice** on federal tax matters

**Does NOT include:** Preparing returns without representing before IRS

flashcards.front: What is an Enrolled Agent and what are their practice rights?
flashcards.back:
**Enrolled Agent (EA):**
A person enrolled to practice before the IRS

**Practice Rights:**
• **Unlimited** practice rights
• Can represent any taxpayer
• On any tax matter
• Before all IRS divisions

**To become EA:**
• Pass 3-part SEE exam, OR
• Former IRS employee (5 years of experience)
• Pass background check and obtain PTIN

flashcards.front: What is the due diligence standard under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Practitioner must exercise due diligence:**

• In **preparing** or assisting with documents
• In **determining correctness** of representations
• In **obtaining correct** information from client
• In **relying** on information furnished by client

**Standard:** May rely on information unless it is obviously incorrect

flashcards.front: What are the "Best Practices" under Circular 230 §10.33?
flashcards.back:
**Best Practices (advisory, not mandatory):**

• Communicate clearly with client about engagement
• Establish facts, evaluate law
• Advise client of **importance of conclusions**
• Act fairly and with integrity

**Managing partners** should ensure firm has adequate procedures for compliance

flashcards.front: What is Form 2848 (Power of Attorney)?
flashcards.back:
**Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative**

**Authorizes representative to:**
• Receive and inspect confidential information
• **Represent** taxpayer before IRS
• Perform any and all acts taxpayer can perform
• Sign agreements, consents, waivers

**Must specify:** Tax matters and years/periods covered

flashcards.front: What is Form 8821 (Tax Information Authorization)?
flashcards.back:
**Tax Information Authorization (TIA)**

**Authorizes designee to:**
• Receive and inspect confidential tax information
• Receive copies of notices and communications

**Does NOT authorize:**
• Representing taxpayer before IRS
• Signing agreements
• Making binding decisions

**Use when:** Client wants someone to receive info but not represent them

flashcards.front: What is a CAF Number?
flashcards.back:
**Centralized Authorization File Number**

**Purpose:**
• Unique number assigned to representatives
• Used to identify authorized representatives
• Linked to all POA/TIA authorizations on file

**Obtained:** Automatically assigned when first valid Form 2848 or 8821 is processed

**Note:** One number per representative, used for ALL clients

flashcards.front: What are the three types of IRS examinations?
flashcards.back:
**1. Correspondence Audit**
• Conducted by mail
• Limited issues (e.g., specific deduction)

**2. Office Audit**
• Taxpayer comes to IRS office
• More complex than correspondence

**3. Field Audit**
• Agent visits taxpayer location
• Most comprehensive
• Usually higher-income/complex returns

flashcards.front: What are the assessment statutes of limitations?
flashcards.back:
**Standard:** 3 years from later of due date or filing date

**Extended to 6 years:**
• Gross income understatement >25%

**Unlimited (no SOL):**
• No return filed
• Fraud
• False return with intent to evade

**Special:** FBAR violations - 6 years
**Extension:** Form 872/872-A

flashcards.front: What is the typical IRS collection notice sequence?
flashcards.back:
**Collection Notice Progression:**

• **CP14** - Initial balance due notice
• **CP501** - Reminder, balance due
• **CP503** - Urgent, immediate payment required
• **CP504** - Notice of Intent to Levy (refunds/state)
• **LT11/Letter 1058** - Final Notice of Intent to Levy and CDP rights

**30-day window** before levy after final notice

flashcards.front: What is a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing?
flashcards.back:
**Collection Due Process Hearing:**

**When available:**
• After Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL)
• After Final Notice of Intent to Levy

**Request deadline:** 30 days from notice date

**Can raise:**
• Spousal defenses
• Collection alternatives (IA, OIC, CNC)
• Appropriateness of collection action
• Underlying liability (if no prior opportunity)

flashcards.front: What are the types of Installment Agreements (IA)?
flashcards.back:
**Guaranteed IA (must approve):**
• Balance ≤$10,000
• Filed/paid all returns last 5 years
• Pay within 3 years

**Streamlined IA:**
• Individual: ≤$50,000, 72 months
• Business: ≤$25,000, 24 months

**Non-Streamlined:**
• >$50,000 or complex situations
• Requires financial disclosure (Form 433-A/B)

**Form 9465** - IA request

flashcards.front: What is an Offer in Compromise (OIC)?
flashcards.back:
**Agreement to settle tax debt for less than full amount**

**Three grounds:**
1. **Doubt as to Liability** - Dispute amount owed
2. **Doubt as to Collectibility** - Cannot pay full amount
3. **Effective Tax Administration** - Hardship/equity

**Forms:** 433-A/433-B (financial), 656 (offer)
**Fee:** $205 application fee (low-income waiver available)
**Payment options:** Lump sum (5 months) or periodic

flashcards.front: What are accuracy-related penalties under §6662?
flashcards.back:
**Accuracy-Related Penalty = 20% of underpayment**

**Applies to:**
• **Negligence** or disregard of rules
• **Substantial understatement** of income tax
• **Substantial valuation** misstatement
• **Substantial overstatement** of pension liabilities
• Any undisclosed foreign financial asset understatement

**Substantial understatement:**
• Individual: Greater of $5,000 or 10% of correct tax

flashcards.front: What is the civil fraud penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Fraud Penalty = 75% of underpayment**

**Elements:**
• Intentional wrongdoing
• Specific intent to evade tax

**Burden of proof:** IRS must prove fraud by **clear and convincing evidence**

**No statute of limitations** for fraud assessments

**Cannot stack** with accuracy-related penalty on same portion

flashcards.front: What is reasonable cause for penalty abatement?
flashcards.back:
**Defense against penalties if:**

• Taxpayer exercised **ordinary business care and prudence**
• Still could not comply with obligation

**Common grounds:**
• Death or serious illness
• Unavoidable absence
• Fire, casualty, natural disaster
• Inability to obtain records
• Reliance on professional advice (documented)

**Form 843** or written statement to request abatement

flashcards.front: What is the IRS Independent Office of Appeals?
flashcards.back:
**Independent Office of Appeals:**

**Purpose:** Resolve tax disputes without litigation

**Jurisdiction:**
• Examination disagreements
• Collection disputes
• Penalty appeals
• Offer in Compromise rejections

**Request via:** Form 12203 (small case <$25K) or written protest

**Appeals are based on:** Hazards of litigation

flashcards.front: What is the U.S. Tax Court?
flashcards.back:
**U.S. Tax Court:**

**Key features:**
• **No prepayment** required - sue before paying
• File petition within 90 days of deficiency notice
• Judges are tax specialists
• Small case procedure for disputes ≤$50,000

**Alternative forums (require prepayment):**
• U.S. District Court
• U.S. Court of Federal Claims

flashcards.front: What sanctions can be imposed under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**Sanctions for Circular 230 Violations:**

• **Private reprimand**
• **Public censure**
• **Suspension** from practice (fixed period)
• **Disbarment** from practice
• **Monetary penalty** (up to gross income from conduct)

**OPR (Office of Professional Responsibility)** handles disciplinary matters

**Due process:** Practitioner entitled to hearing before ALJ

flashcards.front: Section 6694: Understatement due to Unreasonable Position
flashcards.back: Greater of $1,000 or 50% of income derived by preparer for the return.

flashcards.front: Section 6694: Willful or Reckless Conduct
flashcards.back: Greater of $5,000 or 75% of income derived by preparer.

flashcards.front: Section 6695: Failure to Furnish Copy to Taxpayer
flashcards.back: $60 per failure (max $30,000/year for 2024)

flashcards.front: Section 6695: Failure to Sign Return
flashcards.back: $60 per failure (max $30,000/year for 2024)

flashcards.front: Section 6713: Disclosure/Use of Information
flashcards.back: $250 per disclosure (max $10,000/year). Criminal penalty also possible (misdemeanor).

flashcards.front: Referencing EITC Due Diligence Form
flashcards.back: Form 8867. Must be filed with return if claiming EITC, CTC, AOTC, or HOH.

flashcards.front: Record Retention for EITC Due Diligence
flashcards.back: Must keep records for 3 years from due date (or filing date) of return.

flashcards.front: POA (Form 2848) vs TIA (Form 8821)
flashcards.back: POA: Representation + Access info. TIA: Information access ONLY (no representation).

flashcards.front: Duration of POA
flashcards.back: Remains in effect until revoked by taxpayer, withdrawn by rep, or expires (if date set). Death of taxpayer revokes POA.

flashcards.front: Conflict of Interest Retention
flashcards.back: Must keep written consent waiving conflict for 36 months.

flashcards.front: Contingent Fees Rule
flashcards.back: Generally prohibited for origination of tax return. Content allowed for: Amending return challenging IRS exam, Claim for refund (penalty/interest), Judicial proceeding.

flashcards.front: Return of Client Records
flashcards.back: Must return client records needed to comply with tax obligations even if fee unpaid. Can only withhold prepared return (if state law allows).

flashcards.front: Solicitation/Advertising Record Retention
flashcards.back: Must retain copies of ad/communication for 36 months.

flashcards.front: Specified Tax Return Preparer
flashcards.back: Preparer who prepares 11+ covered returns (individuals, trusts, estates) in calendar year must e-file.

flashcards.front: Centralized Authorization File (CAF) No.
flashcards.back: Unique number assigned to identify representatives (agents, attorneys, CPAs, etc.). Not same as PTIN.

flashcards.front: 30-Day Letter
flashcards.back: Proposed adjustments report. Taxpayer has 30 days to agree or file protest (appeal).

flashcards.front: 90-Day Letter (Statutory Notice of Deficiency)
flashcards.back: Issued if 30-day letter ignored/unresolved. Must file Tax Court petition within 90 days (150 if outside US). No extension.

flashcards.front: Small Case Request (Appeals)
flashcards.back: Total amount < $25,000 per period. Formal written protest not required, just simple request.

flashcards.front: US Tax Court
flashcards.back: Only court where tax does NOT need to be paid first. No jury.

flashcards.front: US District Court / Court of Federal Claims
flashcards.back: Must pay tax first and sue for refund. District court is only one with Jury Trial.

flashcards.front: Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED)
flashcards.back: IRS generally has 10 years to collect assessed tax.

flashcards.front: Guaranteed Installment Agreement
flashcards.back: Owe < $10k, filed last 5 years returns, pay in full in 3 years. IRS must accept.

flashcards.front: Streamlined Installment Agreement
flashcards.back: Owe < $50k (total balance), pay in full 72 months (6 years). Financial statement not required.

flashcards.front: Offer in Compromise (OIC) Grounds
flashcards.back:
1. Doubt as to Liability
2. Doubt as to Collectibility
3. Effective Tax Administration (Hardship)

flashcards.front: Currently Not Collectible (CNC)
flashcards.back: Status where IRS pauses collection because taxpayer cannot pay living expenses. Debt remains, interest accrues.

flashcards.front: Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP)
flashcards.back: 100% penalty on responsible persons for willful failure to collect/pay trust fund taxes (payroll w/h). Cannot be discharged in bankruptcy.

flashcards.front: CAP (Collection Appeals Program)
flashcards.back: Quick appeal for specific actions (Lien, Levy, Seizure, Termination of IA). Decision is final (no court appeal).

flashcards.front: CDP (Collection Due Process)
flashcards.back: Available after Final Notice of Intent to Levy or NFTL. Must file within 30 days. Can go to Tax Court.

flashcards.front: Failure to File Penalty
flashcards.back: 5% per month (max 25%). Returns >60 days late: min $510 or 100% tax.

flashcards.front: Failure to Pay Penalty
flashcards.back: 0.5% per month (max 25%). Rate reduced if in Installment Agreement (0.25%) or increased after notice (1%).

flashcards.front: Failure to File vs Failure to Pay
flashcards.back: If both apply, Failure to File (5%) is reduced by Failure to Pay (0.5%) = Net 4.5% for File + 0.5% for Pay = 5% total/month.

flashcards.front: Record Retention - General Rule
flashcards.back: 3 years from return due date or filing date (whichever later).

flashcards.front: Electronic Storage Requirements
flashcards.back: Must ensure integrity, reliability, and readability. Must be able to retrieve and print.

flashcards.front: How is the failure to file penalty calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Failure to File (FTF) Penalty:**

**Rate:** 5% of unpaid tax per month (or part of month)
**Maximum:** 25% of unpaid tax
**Minimum:** Lesser of $485 or 100% of tax (if >60 days late)

**Reduction:** FTF reduced by FTP if both apply

**When assessed together:**
FTF 5% - FTP 0.5% = Net 4.5% per month

flashcards.front: How is the failure to pay penalty calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Failure to Pay (FTP) Penalty:**

**Rate:** 0.5% of unpaid tax per month
**Maximum:** 25% of unpaid tax

**Increases to 1%:** If IRS issues levy notice and 10 days passes without payment

**Decreases to 0.25%:** If taxpayer has installment agreement in place

flashcards.front: What is the maximum combined FTF and FTP penalty?
flashcards.back:
**Combined Maximum: 47.5%**

**Calculation:**
• FTF max: 25% (over 5 months)
• FTP max: 25% (over 50 months)
• But FTF is reduced by FTP: Net FTF = 22.5%
• Combined: 22.5% + 25% = **47.5%**

**Timeline for maximum:**
• First 5 months: FTF dominates (4.5% net + 0.5% FTP = 5%)
• After 5 months: FTP only (0.5%/month until maxed)

flashcards.front: What constitutes a "substantial understatement"?
flashcards.back:
**Substantial Understatement (Individuals):**

Greater of:
• $5,000, OR
• 10% of correct tax

**Substantial Understatement (Corporations):**

Greater of:
• $10,000, OR
• 10% of correct tax (or 5% if tax >$10 million)

**Penalty:** 20% of underpayment
flashcards.formula: Substantial = Greater of $5,000 or 10% of correct tax

flashcards.front: How is the underpayment of estimated tax penalty calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Estimated Tax Penalty (IRC §6654):**

**Applies if:** Required annual payment not met through withholding + estimates

**Required payment (lesser of):**
• 90% of current year tax, OR
• 100% of prior year tax (110% if AGI >$150,000)

**Penalty rate:** Federal short-term rate + 3%

**Calculated quarterly:** Jan 15, Apr 15, Jun 15, Sep 15

flashcards.front: How is IRS interest on underpayments calculated?
flashcards.back:
**Underpayment Interest Rate:**

Federal short-term rate + 3%

**Compounded daily** from due date until paid

**Corporate rates:**
• Standard: Short-term + 3%
• Large corporate underpayments: Short-term + 5%

**Overpayment (refund) rate:**
• Short-term + 3% (individuals)
• Short-term + 2% (corporations)

flashcards.front: When does IRS interest start and stop running?
flashcards.back:
**Interest on underpayment:**
• **Starts:** Original due date (without extensions)
• **Stops:** Date of payment

**Interest on overpayment (refund):**
• **Starts:** Later of due date or payment date
• **Stops:** Date refund issued
• **No interest if:** Refund within 45 days of filing

**Suspension:** No interest during certain delays by IRS (§6404(g))
flashcards.formula: Interest period = Due date → Payment date

flashcards.front: What are all the assessment statute of limitations periods?
flashcards.back:
**Assessment Periods:**

• **3 years:** Standard (from later of due date or filing)
• **6 years:** >25% gross income omission; FBAR violations
• **Unlimited:** No return filed, fraud, false return

**Collection Statute:**
• **10 years** from date of assessment (CSED)

**Refund Claim:**
• Later of 3 years from filing or 2 years from payment

flashcards.front: How does the IRS calculate reasonable collection potential (RCP) for OICs?
flashcards.back:
**Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP):**

**RCP = Net Equity in Assets + Future Income**

**Net Equity:**
FMV of assets × 80% (quick sale value) − Encumbrances

**Future Income:**
• Lump Sum: Monthly disposable income × 12
• Periodic: Monthly disposable income × 24

**OIC must equal or exceed RCP to be accepted**
flashcards.example: Assets QSV $20K, Monthly disposable $500, Lump Sum:\nRCP = $20,000 + ($500 × 12) = $26,000
flashcards.formula: RCP = Quick Sale Value + (Monthly Income × 12 or 24)

flashcards.front: What expenses does IRS allow when calculating disposable income?
flashcards.back:
**IRS Collection Financial Standards:**

**National Standards (fixed):**
• Food, clothing, personal care
• Out-of-pocket health care

**Local Standards (varies by location):**
• Housing and utilities
• Transportation (ownership + operating)

**Other Necessary Expenses (actual):**
• Court-ordered payments
• Dependent care
• Life insurance (term only)

**Disposable = Income − Allowable Expenses**
flashcards.formula: Disposable Income = Gross − National − Local − Other Necessary

flashcards.front: What are the tax return preparer penalties?
flashcards.back:
**Return Preparer Penalties:**

**§6694(a) - Unreasonable Position:**
• Greater of $1,000 or 50% of income derived
• No reasonable basis (disclosed) or
• No substantial authority (undisclosed)

**§6694(b) - Willful/Reckless:**
• Greater of $5,000 or 75% of income derived

**§6695 - Other violations:**
• Failure to sign: $60
• Failure to furnish copy to taxpayer: $60
• Failure to keep copy/list: $60
• Failure to include PTIN: $60
flashcards.formula: Unreasonable: Greater of $1,000 or 50% of fees\nWillful: Greater of $5,000 or 75% of fees

flashcards.front: What are the penalties for failure to file information returns?
flashcards.back:
**Information Return Penalties (§6721):**

**Based on when corrected:**
• Within 30 days: $60/return
• Between 31 days and Aug 1: $130/return
• After Aug 1 or not corrected: $330/return

**Intentional disregard:** $660/return (no maximum)

**Small business max ($6M or less):**
• 30-day: $230,500
• 31-Aug 1: $693,500
• After Aug 1: $1,387,000
flashcards.formula: Penalty increases based on: 30-day, Aug 1, Never corrected

flashcards.front: What are the dollar thresholds for different appeal procedures?
flashcards.back:
**Appeals Process Thresholds:**

**Small Case Request (Form 12203):**
• Total amount ≤$25,000 for any one tax period

**Written Protest Required:**
• Total amount >$25,000
• Must include statement of facts, law, arguments

**Tax Court Small Case:**
• Amount in dispute ≤$50,000
• Decision is final (no appeal)
flashcards.formula: <$25K: Form 12203 | >$25K: Written Protest | <$50K: Small Tax Court

flashcards.front: What is the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP)?
flashcards.back:
**Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP):**

**Amount:** 100% of trust fund taxes not remitted

**Trust fund taxes:**
• Income tax withheld from employees
• Employee share of FICA

**NOT trust fund:** Employer share of FICA, FUTA

**Who is liable:**
• "Responsible person" who
• "Willfully" failed to collect/pay

**Personal liability** - assessed against individuals

flashcards.front: What are the installment agreement thresholds and fees?
flashcards.back:
**Installment Agreement Thresholds:**

**Guaranteed (must approve):**
• Balance ≤$10,000
• All returns filed for 5 years
• Full payment in 36 months

**Streamlined:**
• Individual: ≤$50,000 (72 months)
• Business: ≤$25,000 (24 months)

**Setup Fees (2024):**
• Online/Direct Debit: $31
• Phone/Mail/Direct Debit: $107
• Standard: $178
• Low-income: $43 (may be reimbursed)

flashcards.front: What are a practitioner\
flashcards.back:
**"DIRECT"** - Practitioner Duties:

• **D**ue diligence in preparing documents
• **I**nformation - furnish to IRS promptly
• **R**eturns - submit accurately and timely
• **E**vidence of error - advise client of consequences
• **C**onflicts of interest - avoid or disclose
• **T**imely response to IRS requests

flashcards.front: What conduct is prohibited under Circular 230?
flashcards.back:
**"FINES"** - Prohibited Conduct:

• **F**alse or misleading statements
• **I**ncompetence or disreputable conduct
• **N**eglecting client matters
• **E**ngaging in frivolous arguments
• **S**oliciting improperly (in-person for profit)

**Also:** Charging unconscionable fees, cashing IRS checks

flashcards.front: What are the Circular 230 sanctions in order of severity?
flashcards.back:
**"REPS-MD"** - Sanctions (least to most severe):

• **R**eprimand (private)
• **E**nsure (public censure)
• **P**robation (practice with conditions)
• **S**uspension (temporary loss of privilege)
• **M**onetary penalty
• **D**isbarment (permanent loss of privilege)

**OPR** (Office of Professional Responsibility) imposes sanctions

flashcards.front: How to remember the difference between Form 2848 and Form 8821?
flashcards.back:
**"2848 = TWO-way, 8821 = ONE-way"**

**Form 2848 (POA):**
• **TWO-way:** Can SEND AND RECEIVE to IRS
• Represent, sign, advocate
• Full practice rights

**Form 8821 (TIA):**
• **ONE-way:** Can only RECEIVE from IRS
• Information only
• No representation power

**Memory tip:** 2848 has more digits = more power

flashcards.front: Who can practice before the IRS with unlimited rights?
flashcards.back:
**"ACE"** - Unlimited Practice Rights:

• **A**ttorneys (state bar membership)
• **C**PAs (state CPA license)
• **E**nrolled Agents (pass SEE exam)

**Limited Rights:**
• Enrolled Retirement Plan Agents (ERPAs)
• Enrolled Actuaries
• AFSP holders (only prepared returns)
• Annual Filing Season Program

flashcards.front: What are the three types of IRS audits?
flashcards.back:
**"COF"** - Audit Types (simple to complex):

• **C**orrespondence - Mail only, simple issues
• **O**ffice - Taxpayer goes to IRS office
• **F**ield - Agent visits taxpayer location

**Complexity increases C → O → F**

**Selection methods:** DIF score, random, referral, related return

flashcards.front: What are the three courts for tax disputes?
flashcards.back:
**"TDF"** - Tax Courts (before to after payment):

• **T**ax Court - Sue BEFORE paying (90-day letter)
• **D**istrict Court - Sue AFTER paying (jury available)
• **F**ederal Claims Court - Sue AFTER paying (no jury)

**Key:** Only Tax Court allows prepayment litigation

**Appeal routes:**
• Tax Court/District → Circuit Court of Appeals
• Federal Claims → Federal Circuit

flashcards.front: What is the collection notice sequence?
flashcards.back:
**"14-501-503-504-LT11"** Notice Sequence:

• **CP14** - Initial balance due
• **CP501** - First reminder
• **CP503** - Second/urgent reminder
• **CP504** - Intent to levy (state refund/passport)
• **LT11** - Final Notice, CDP rights

**30 days after LT11** → Levy can begin

flashcards.front: What can taxpayers raise at a CDP hearing?
flashcards.back:
**"SCRAP"** - CDP Hearing Issues:

• **S**pousal defenses (innocent spouse)
• **C**ollection alternatives (IA, OIC, CNC)
• **R**eason for action appropriateness
• **A**mount of liability (if no prior opportunity)
• **P**rocedural errors by IRS

**Must request within 30 days** of notice

flashcards.front: What are the three grounds for an Offer in Compromise?
flashcards.back:
**"LCD"** - OIC Grounds:

• **L**iability doubt - Dispute you owe
• **C**ollectibility doubt - Cannot afford to pay
• **D**isruption (Effective Tax Admin) - Payment would cause hardship

**Most common:** Doubt as to Collectibility

**RCP Formula:** Quick sale value assets + Future income

flashcards.front: How to remember FTF vs FTP penalty rates?
flashcards.back:
**"5 is BIGGER than 0.5 - File is BIGGER than Pay"**

• **F**ailure to **F**ile: **5%** per month (max 25%)
• **F**ailure to **P**ay: **0.5%** per month (max 25%)

**Memory:** The FIRST letter penalty (File) is the BIGGER one

**When both apply:** FTF reduced by FTP amount

flashcards.front: How to remember accuracy-related penalty percentages?
flashcards.back:
**"20-40-75"** Accuracy Penalty Tiers:

• **20%** - Accuracy-related (§6662)
  - Negligence, substantial understatement
  - Substantial valuation misstatement

• **40%** - Gross valuation misstatement
  - 200% or more overstatement

• **75%** - Civil fraud (§6663)
  - Must prove by clear and convincing evidence

flashcards.front: What are common reasonable cause defenses for penalty abatement?
flashcards.back:
**"FIRE DUCK"** - Reasonable Cause:

• **F**ire or casualty
• **I**llness (death, serious medical)
• **R**eliance on professional advice
• **E**rror by IRS or advisor

• **D**eath in immediate family
• **U**navoidable absence
• **C**annot obtain records
• **K**nowledge lacking (good faith, first-time)

**Form 843** for refund claims including penalty abatement

flashcards.front: How to remember assessment statute of limitations periods?
flashcards.back:
**"3-6-Forever"** Assessment SOL:

• **3** years - Standard (from later of due date or filing)
• **6** years - >25% gross income omission; FBAR
• **Forever** - No return, fraud, false return

**Collection SOL:** 10 years from assessment (CSED)

**Refund:** Later of 3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment

flashcards.front: What are the 10 Taxpayer Rights (Taxpayer Bill of Rights)?
flashcards.back:
**"I PREFER QAC"** - Taxpayer Bill of Rights:

• **I**nformed - Right to be informed
• **P**rivacy - Right to privacy
• **R**epresent - Right to representation
• **E**xplain - Right to know why IRS is asking for info
• **F**inal say - Right to finality
• **E**asy system - Right to a fair and just tax system
• **R**etain service - Right to retain representation

• **Q**uality - Right to quality service
• **A**ppeal - Right to appeal in independent forum
• **C**hallenge - Right to challenge IRS position and be heard
